Apply the **scientific method** and maintain analytic rigor, ensuring simple explanations are backed by provable means, not just convenience.
What specific evidence or single factor would disprove S, and have we actively looked for it?
What assumptions are embedded in S, and what other models, if applied, might expose a necessary layer of complexity that S is concealing?

treatment_requirements.description: Before preferring the simplest explanation, compare the competing explanations by the assumptions and variables each one adds while holding them accountable to the same observed facts.
evidence_required: The candidate explanations or hypotheses being compared.
The assumptions, variables, or unproven premises each explanation requires.
The observed facts or predictions that each explanation must account for.
good_output_shape: Names the explanation with the fewest unsupported assumptions.
Shows which assumptions or variables were shaved off and why they did not change the decision.
Keeps the chosen explanation provisional until further evidence changes the comparison.

treatment_requirements.description: Stress-test the lean explanation against hard evidence, disconfirming observations, and countermodels before letting parsimony override necessary causal structure.
evidence_required: The hard evidence or data that would challenge the simpler explanation.
The disconfirming factor or observation that would require a more elaborate explanation.
The other models, system interactions, or analytic tools that may reveal necessary complexity.
good_output_shape: Separates evidence-based simplicity from a merely neat or comfortable story.
States the factor that would disprove the simple explanation or force a more complex account.
Preserves necessary complexity when simpler framing would hide the mechanism.

case_evidence_needed: Evidence that the current frame is binary or prematurely narrowed.
A list of at least three viable options before evaluation begins.
A comparison basis for benefits, costs, payoffs, or probabilities across options.
do_not_use_when: The option set is already large enough that further expansion is causing paralysis.
The decision is routine or inconsequential enough that option analysis would waste attention.
The team is seeking a perfect option instead of making a needed decision.
use_when: An important decision is framed as only do or do not.
The problem is complex enough that premature selection may hide better alternatives.
The current recommendation lacks a visible array of viable paths.

diagnostic_questions: What viable third option breaks the do-or-do-not frame?
Have we generated at least three options before selecting among them?
Which benefits, costs, payoffs, or probabilities differ across the options?
affordances.mechanism: When an important decision has collapsed into a do-or-do-not frame, separate invention from evaluation: generate at least three viable options, then compare benefits, costs, payoffs, and probabilities before choosing.
misuse_guards: Stop expanding the set when too many attractive choices are preventing any decision.
Do not spend meaningful analysis on routine, inconsequential choices.
Do not keep analyzing in pursuit of a perfect option when action is needed.
affordances.name: Create a non-binary option set before selection

source_evidence.source_quote: the tendency to simplify a complex issue down to just two options (e.g., "do" or "do not").
requiring the decision-maker to generate at least three options (3+) for every important decision.
generate many options before selecting among them
requires quantifying benefits and costs across an array of options.
decision trees can be used, particularly if some choices are probabilistic in nature.
When faced with too many attractive options, people can become paralyzed and fail to make a decision at all.
Spending significant time analyzing options for routine, inconsequential decisions (e.g., what to eat, watch, or wear) wastes enormous amounts of time
The goal is not to analyze everything to achieve perfection

treatment_requirements.description: Delay selection long enough to invent multiple viable paths, with at least three options visible before the recommendation narrows.
evidence_required: Current binary framing or prematurely narrowed alternatives.
A generated option set with at least three viable paths.
Criteria that explain why any option is retained or eliminated.
good_output_shape: Names the expanded option set before recommending a path.
Explains which options remain viable and which are dominated.
Shows that selection happened after invention, not before it.

treatment_requirements.description: Evaluate the option array by comparing benefits, costs, payoffs, and probabilistic branches rather than using a simple pro-con list.
evidence_required: Benefits and costs for each serious option.
Relevant payoffs, probabilities, or branches where uncertainty matters.
Explicit trade-offs that distinguish the options from each other.
good_output_shape: Presents a compact comparison across the option array.
Identifies the option with the best supported upside-to-downside profile.
Names uncertainty that should change the final selection.

case_evidence_needed: A clear statement of what makes the choice reversible or irreversible.
A cheap experiment or staged action that buys information before final commitment.
A downside cap and a decision boundary for when enough information exists to commit.
do_not_use_when: Preserving options delays the commitment needed to capture time-sensitive value.
Maintaining optionality consumes cash, attention, or simplicity better spent on the chosen path.
The worst case is too severe to tolerate even as a staged experiment.
use_when: Uncertainty is high, early commitment is expensive, and cheap experiments can buy learning.
The decision is reversible enough that moving now creates information value.
Downside can be capped while upside remains open across plausible futures.

diagnostic_questions: What cheap reversible move buys information before the irreversible call?
Is the worst case bad enough?
Would we regret making or not making this decision later?
Do we have between 40% and 70% of the information needed to decide?
affordances.mechanism: When uncertainty is high and commitment is expensive, keep several reversible paths open long enough to run cheap learning moves, cap downside, and commit once evidence makes delay more costly than choice.
misuse_guards: Do not let optionality become a permanent excuse not to commit after evidence clarifies best fit.
Do not preserve paths whose carrying cost is consuming cash, attention, or simplicity.
Do not treat every decision as irreversible; that assumption can create indecision.
affordances.name: Preserve reversible paths to buy learning

source_evidence.source_quote: uncertainty is high, early commitment is expensive, and cheap experiments can buy learning before an irreversible call.
decisions are reversible enough that preserving multiple paths creates information value rather than just indecision.
downside can be capped while upside remains open across several plausible futures.
there is nothing to lose and only information and speed to gain.
Make a decision when you have **no less than 40% of the information** you need but **no more than 70%**.
optionality becomes a permanent excuse not to commit even after evidence has clarified which path has the best economics or strategic fit.
maintaining optionality is itself consuming cash, attention, or organizational simplicity that would be better spent on a chosen path.
Is the worst case bad enough?
asks if they would regret making (or not making) a specific decision.
Assuming all decisions are irreversible causes indecision.

treatment_requirements.description: Classify reversibility, then choose the cheapest staged move that can reduce uncertainty before the irreversible call.
evidence_required: What would make the decision irreversible or expensive to reverse.
The specific learning the reversible move is expected to produce.
The capped downside if the staged move fails.
good_output_shape: Separates reversible learning moves from irreversible commitment.
Names the experiment, expected information, and downside cap.
Keeps upside open while making the next action concrete.

treatment_requirements.description: Define when optionality expires, using an information threshold, deadline, or clarified strategic fit to force commitment.
evidence_required: Current level of information relative to the decision threshold.
Cost of delaying commitment in cash, attention, simplicity, or timing.
Evidence that would prove one path has the best economics or strategic fit.
good_output_shape: States the commitment trigger before preserving more options.
Commits once evidence has clarified the best-supported path.
Treats optionality as temporary learning capacity, not permanent deferral.

case_evidence_needed: The milestone where reverse cost rises above the counterparty's cost to lose the relationship.
Specific lock-in sources such as integration, retraining, data ownership, escalation terms, renewal structure, migration, or service dependencies.
Any reversible exit path or live fallback lane that can preserve leverage before the commitment becomes binding.
do_not_use_when: Hold back when execution quality, incentives, product reality, regulation, or a physical bottleneck explains the constraint better than leverage.
Do not count a signed contract as durable control without checking the next operational commitment.
use_when: A contract, integration, migration, financing, or operating commitment could make exit materially harder after the deal is signed.
The current party looks strong before signature but may become dependent once operational lock-in, training, or customer migration begins.

diagnostic_questions: At what milestone does our cost to reverse become higher than the counterparty's cost to lose us?
Where does integration, training, financing, or customer migration raise the cost to reverse?
What commitment should stay reversible until leverage-protection terms are secured?
affordances.mechanism: Leverage can flip after signature when implementation, training, financing, migration, data access, or switching costs make the original walk-away no longer credible.
misuse_guards: Do not treat a signed contract as durable control when the next operational commitment shifts timing, data, or switching-cost leverage.
Do not celebrate headline price concessions if escalation terms, renewal structure, data ownership, or service dependencies invert bargaining power.
affordances.name: Commitment-gradient leverage inversion

source_evidence.source_quote: Track leverage inversion over time
The side with leverage before signature can lose it after lock-in, integration, training, financing, or customer migration raises the cost to reverse.
a contract, integration, or operating commitment could invert leverage over time so the side that looks strong at signature becomes weak after lock-in.
A buyer wins aggressive terms up front, then loses leverage after implementation starts because exit cost, retraining burden, and contract commitment make the walk-away no longer credible.
At what milestone does our cost to reverse become higher than the counterparty's cost to lose us?
leaders mistake a signed contract for durable control even though the next operational commitment hands timing, data, or switching cost leverage to the counterparty.
negotiating headline price while ignoring the commitment gradient
A team "wins" concessions upfront but gives away escalation terms, renewal structure, data ownership, or service dependencies that later invert bargaining power.
preserve leverage by keeping a live fallback lane and a reversible exit path.

treatment_requirements.description: Before endorsing a commitment, mark the milestone where leverage may invert and specify protections that preserve fallback lanes or reversal rights.
evidence_required: Implementation, integration, retraining, financing, migration, data, renewal, or dependency steps that raise exit costs.
Counterparty leverage gained through timing, data access, escalation terms, service dependencies, or switching costs.
A comparable view of the team's reverse cost and the counterparty's cost to lose the deal at each milestone.
good_output_shape: Shows before-signature and after-commitment leverage separately rather than treating the signed deal as final control.
Names the first lock-in milestone and the mitigation, such as delaying commitment, preserving a fallback lane, or negotiating exit rights.
Warns when headline concessions give away structural control later.

case_evidence_needed: Each party's lowest-cost fallback, including alternate counterparties, channels, delayed purchase, redirected supply, or demand.
Evidence that a threatened walk-away or delay can actually be enforced at tolerable operational, reputation, and approval cost.
Information being withheld about capacity, backlog, demand quality, timing, or reserved alternatives.
do_not_use_when: Do not treat symbolic status, loud positioning, or verbal toughness as leverage unless the fallback is credible.
Hold back when a hard regulatory or physical bottleneck explains the constraint better than bargaining power.
use_when: A negotiation or partnership depends on private capacity, hidden fallback options, or who can truly walk away.
One side is using scarcity language, reserved capacity, timing pressure, or channel access without revealing its alternatives.

diagnostic_questions: Which party can absorb a walk-away with less damage this quarter, and what evidence proves it?
What would make our walk-away or delay threat actually binding rather than rhetorical?
What critical information about capacity, fallback demand, or timing is being strategically withheld, and by whom?
affordances.mechanism: Terms should be treated as downstream of each party's fallback, walk-away credibility, and ability to redirect, delay, or absorb damage.
misuse_guards: Do not count verbal toughness as leverage when the fallback is worse than the current deal.
Do not treat titles, confidence, or loud positioning as real bargaining power without a better outside option.
affordances.name: Outside-option credibility before terms

source_evidence.source_quote: Power Dynamics is applied by asking who can walk away, who can wait, which commitment is binding, and where the hidden outside option actually sits.
Map outside options before discussing terms
If one side can redirect supply, shift channels, delay purchase, or accept a different counterparty at lower cost, that outside option is part of the real price floor.
Separate stated willingness from credible walk-away
A threat only changes bargaining power when the other side believes it will actually be carried out and that the cost of enforcing it is tolerable.
a negotiation depends on private capacity, hidden fallback options, or information asymmetry about who can truly walk away without major damage.
a buyer, supplier, or partner is using scarcity language, reserved capacity, or channel access to shape the negotiation without fully revealing the underlying fallback alternatives.
a team treats verbal toughness as leverage even though the walk-away is not credible and the fallback option is worse than the current deal.
Titles, confidence, or loud positioning do not create leverage if the other side has the better outside option.
Which party can absorb a walk-away with less damage this quarter, and what evidence proves it?
What would make our walk-away or delay threat actually binding rather than rhetorical?
What critical information about capacity, fallback demand, or timing is being strategically withheld, and by whom?

treatment_requirements.description: Before recommending terms, build the leverage map: who can wait, walk away, redirect demand or supply, and absorb enforcement costs with less damage.
evidence_required: Credible alternatives for each party, including redirecting supply, shifting channels, delaying purchase, or accepting a different counterparty.
Proof that the proposed walk-away or delay threat can be carried out at a tolerable cost.
Signals that scarcity, capacity, channel access, or timing claims are real rather than manufactured leverage.
good_output_shape: Names the party with the stronger outside option and the specific evidence supporting that judgment.
Separates stated willingness from credible walk-away and explains what would make a threat binding rather than rhetorical.
Treats price floor, timing, or service level as downstream of the mapped bargaining position.

case_evidence_needed: The current plan or thesis, including the success conditions that must line up.
Implicit assumptions the team would have to believe for the plan to hold.
Plausible failure causes, downstream domino effects, and the worst-case outcome.
Which surfaced risks are controllable enough to change before launch.
do_not_use_when: The plan is already committed and premortem language would only become after-the-fact criticism theater.
The team will list worries without assigning owners, mitigations, or decision changes.
use_when: A project or decision is still pre-commitment and failure would be costly.
Optimism, social pressure, or executive momentum is making a favored plan feel safer than it is.
Success depends on multiple conditions lining up and weak links or contingency gaps need surfacing.

diagnostic_questions: What would you have to believe for the current plan to work?
Is the worst case bad enough to change the plan or commitment level?
Do we still remember the question we are trying to answer, or have we substituted an easier one?
affordances.mechanism: Before commitment, treat the plan as already failed, work backward through plausible causes and domino effects, then convert controllable failure paths into mitigations, owners, reversal thresholds, or decision changes.
misuse_guards: Do not treat a generic list of worries as a completed premortem unless it changes ownership, mitigations, or decisions.
Do not replace the backward failure simulation with a pro-con list that makes positives more available than failure causes.
Do not use the model after commitment as criticism theater when the team is unwilling to alter the plan.
affordances.name: Simulated failure converted into plan changes

dropped_material: Did not split systems-thinking, second-order-thinking, competitive strategy, Digital Twins, or organizational design into separate affordances because they support or contextualize the same core premortem operation in this source.
review_notes.normalization_note: Condensed the raw source and reviewed curation into one operational contract: simulate failure before commitment, identify plausible causes and domino effects, then bind actionable risks to plan changes.
open_questions: Future relation-aware review could decide whether second-order-thinking deserves a separate affordance rather than remaining inside this treatment requirement.

source_evidence.source_quote: Before the project begins, the team gathers and assumes that the project has failed spectacularly. They then spend time detailing the plausible reasons *why* it failed, operating under the psychological advantage of hindsight.

Instead of just considering the first step or immediate consequence (e.g., "We launch the product"), the Premortem forces visualization of the entire chain of consequences, or **"all the dominoes"** that could fall, extending beyond immediate, self-imposed consequences.
This involves meticulously spelling out all the implicit assumptions inherent in the project plan and pressure-testing them against reality.
**Most useful when** a project is still pre-commitment and the cost of discovering failure modes early is much lower than discovering them after launch.
**Works best when** social pressure, optimism, or executive momentum would otherwise suppress dissent and make obvious-looking plans feel safer than they are.
**Best used when** success depends on multiple conditions lining up and the team needs to expose weak links, reversibility limits, and contingency gaps in advance.
**Danger when** the exercise produces an obvious list of worries without owners, mitigations, or decision changes tied to what was surfaced.
**Danger when** premortem language is used after commitment only as criticism theater rather than as a pre-decision tool for improving the plan.
A common anti-pattern is substituting the deep, critical work of a Premortem with a simpler, more available tool, such as a basic pro-con list.

Premortem surfaces hidden failure assumptions before escalation by forcing teams to write explicit falsification checks, reversal triggers, and stop-loss thresholds that are binding at checkpoint review.
"Is the worst case bad enough?"
"What would you have to believe?" to accept the current plan's core assumptions.
"Do we still remember the question we are trying to answer? Or have we substituted an easier one?"

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to assume the project has failed before launch and work backward to plausible causes instead of starting from the preferred success case.
evidence_required: A concrete simulated failure state for the project or decision.
Plausible reasons why that failure occurred, including hidden assumptions and weak links.
good_output_shape: Names the simulated failure and the causal path that could produce it.
Maps core assumptions using what-would-you-have-to-believe framing.
Checks whether the worst case is bad enough to alter the decision.

treatment_requirements.description: Require important surfaced risks to be translated into owners, mitigations, reversal triggers, stop-loss thresholds, or explicit decision changes.
evidence_required: The surfaced risk or failure path and whether it can be controlled before launch.
The mitigation, owner, threshold, checkpoint, or decision change that will address it.
good_output_shape: Separates actionable risks from worries that cannot change the plan.
Attaches owners, mitigations, thresholds, or decision changes to retained risks.
Defers runtime use when the output remains an obvious worry list.

case_evidence_needed: The current problem statement and the decision maker whose needs it is meant to serve.
The proposed analysis path, desired output, success criteria, constraints, and actors affected by the problem.
do_not_use_when: The next useful move is commitment and additional reframing is mainly postponing a hard decision.
use_when: The team is working hard but getting weak answers, weak priorities, or repeated confusion.
Analysis is starting before the core question, owner, constraints, actors, and success criteria are explicit.

diagnostic_questions: What is the single core question we are trying to answer?
What output is precisely needed from this analysis to solve the problem?
Is this solution focused on the symptom or the root cause?
affordances.mechanism: Because the chosen frame dictates the solution space, the model requires a precise decision-maker-centered problem statement before expensive analysis or solution work earns weight.
misuse_guards: Do not let reframing become a substitute for commitment.
Do not gather broad data until the frame or hypothesis says what the analysis must prove or disprove.
affordances.name: Define the problem before analysis deepens

source_evidence.source_quote: how a problem is defined dictates the solution space
Problem solving begins with the critical step of defining the problem precisely to meet the decision maker's needs.
A structured framework for rigorous problem definition, ensuring clarity on the **T**rouble, **O**wner, **S**uccess criteria, **C**onstraints, and **A**ctors involved.
the team is working hard on a problem but getting suspiciously weak answers, weak priorities, or repeated confusion.
reframing becomes a substitute for commitment and teams keep changing the question to avoid making a hard decision.

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: Rigorously frame the question or hypothesis before embarking on complex analysis (such as machine learning). Gather *only enough facts* needed to prove or disprove the current hypothesis.

source_evidence.source_quote: What output is precisely needed from this analysis to solve the problem?

treatment_requirements.description: State the core decision-worthy question and the minimum analysis output needed before gathering more data or proposing solutions.
evidence_required: Quote or restate the current problem definition before changing it.
Name the decision maker, the success criteria, constraints, actors, and the analysis output required.
good_output_shape: A precise problem statement tied to the decision maker's need.
A short path from the reframed question to the next analysis or solution decision.

treatment_requirements.description: Delay computation or broad data gathering until the frame specifies what evidence would prove or disprove the current hypothesis.
evidence_required: The current hypothesis or question that analysis is supposed to resolve.
The smallest fact set needed to advance toward the goal.
good_output_shape: A bounded evidence request instead of an open-ended data search.
A clear explanation of how the requested analysis advances the problem-solving path.

case_evidence_needed: The current hypothesis, preferred frame, objection, or assumption-laden viewpoint.
The beliefs that would have to be true and the evidence that would prove the frame wrong.
do_not_use_when: The team only wants evidence that confirms its initial framing or preferred conclusion.
use_when: A preferred hypothesis or initial vision is hardening into the problem frame.
A novel idea looks impossible under old mental models, but the enabling conditions have not yet been specified.

diagnostic_questions: What would need to be true for this idea to work?
What would you have to believe?
What evidence would conclusively prove our current framing/hypothesis wrong?
If we disproved this hypothesis, what would we do next?
affordances.mechanism: When an initial frame or objection is driven by old assumptions, the model converts the frame into enabling conditions, belief commitments, and falsifying evidence before preserving or replacing it.
misuse_guards: Do not let constructive reframing become motivated reasoning for the conclusion the team already wants.
Do not evaluate novel problems only through familiar frames that block alternative causal structures.
affordances.name: Expose assumptions that lock a frame in place

source_evidence.source_quote: What would need to be true for this idea to work?
This reframing allowed them to anticipate and invest in the emergence of a new mental model that eventually made the concept acceptable and profitable.
Confirmation bias drives problem solvers to seek evidence that confirms their initial framing or hypothesis, making them unwilling to investigate or analyze deeply enough to justify a reframing.

source_evidence.section_hint: Common Failure Modes, Cognitive Biases, or Blind Spots
source_evidence.source_quote: The psychological tendency to process information and select a frame that leads to a desired conclusion, rather than one that discovers the truth.

source_evidence.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

source_evidence.source_quote: What evidence would conclusively prove our current framing/hypothesis wrong?

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: If we disproved this hypothesis, what would we do next?
problem solvers sometimes persist in using familiar frames that are unhelpful or misleading, risking disastrously wrong solutions.

treatment_requirements.description: Surface the beliefs underneath the current frame and specify the evidence or future conditions that would validate, invalidate, or replace it.
evidence_required: The current framing or hypothesis and why it is attractive or familiar.
Disconfirming evidence, enabling conditions, and next moves if the hypothesis fails.
good_output_shape: A list of assumptions or would-have-to-believe statements underneath the frame.
A falsification test and a next-frame path if the current hypothesis is disproved.

case_evidence_needed: The initial frame, unit of analysis, and any familiar framework currently shaping the work.
Alternative disciplines, functional frames, logical frameworks, or boundaries that could view the issue differently.
do_not_use_when: The issue has already been tested through distinct frames and the limiting need is execution or decision commitment.
use_when: Multiple plausible frames exist and the cost of choosing the wrong one is high.
The current lens is producing weak priorities, repeated confusion, or a suspiciously narrow unit of analysis.

diagnostic_questions: What other disciplines or frameworks might view this problem entirely differently?
What are the implicit assumptions embedded in the current frame, and are they met in this context?
affordances.mechanism: The model treats each frame as a partial map that reveals some factors and hides others, so a high-cost problem should be cut through several structurally different lenses before a frame is chosen.
misuse_guards: Do not force reality to fit the one framework the team knows best.
Watch for anchoring or substitution bias when one salient cue or familiar framework leads the work.
affordances.name: Test alternative frames before choosing the lens

source_evidence.source_quote: A way of seeing is also inherently a way of not seeing.
Different frames often yield different insights, necessitating the testing of several approaches.
By utilizing a broad **"latticework"** of mental models from various disciplines, one can view a problem in a multi-dimensional way, ensuring the most appropriate frame is selected.
multiple plausible frames exist and the cost of choosing the wrong one is high.

source_evidence.section_hint: Common Failure Modes, Cognitive Biases, or Blind Spots
source_evidence.source_quote: Focusing too much on a single, salient piece of information (the anchor) or substituting a known, simple framework for a complex, necessary one.

treatment_requirements.description: Generate and compare multiple distinct frames, then identify what each frame reveals, hides, assumes, and changes about the unit of analysis.
evidence_required: The current frame and at least two plausible alternative frames.
The assumptions embedded in the current frame and whether those assumptions hold in context.
good_output_shape: A frame comparison that names the different lenses and the distinct insight each produces.
A selected frame or reframed boundary with a reason tied to problem structure, not familiarity.

case_evidence_needed: The decision, action, or system whose threats, challenges, and pitfalls need to be anticipated.
The worst-case scenario factors, hidden failure paths, or disjunctive component failures that could dominate the outcome.
Known unknowns, unknown unknowns, and system interdependencies that could make the current risk picture incomplete.
Concrete mitigations, thresholds, monitoring signals, and safeguards that would change the decision or execution plan.
do_not_use_when: The decision is reversible or low-stakes enough that extended risk work would waste attention.
The team is producing checklists, matrices, or warnings for governance optics without changing decisions or safeguards.
The analysis cannot narrow from all imaginable risks to the biggest-impact drivers the team can influence.
use_when: Downside exposure could dominate the decision and surviving failure matters as much as chasing upside.
Hidden failure paths, low-probability high-impact consequences, or disjunctive system failures could break the plan before visible trade-offs matter.
Uncertainty needs governance through mitigations, thresholds, and monitoring rather than general caution language.

diagnostic_questions: Is the worst case bad enough?
Which hidden failure path or disjunctive component failure could dominate before the visible trade-off matters?
What threshold or monitoring signal would tell us this risk is becoming real enough to change course?
Which risks are we analyzing because they are easy to list, and which few have the biggest impact and are actually influenceable?
affordances.mechanism: Surface hidden failure paths and worst-case drivers, then convert the few material exposures into explicit mitigations, thresholds, monitoring, and safeguards before the plan earns confidence.
misuse_guards: Do not turn risk assessment into a broad inventory; the output must change mitigations, thresholds, monitoring, decisions, or safeguards.
Do not spend heavy analysis on reversible or low-stakes choices where risk work would thwart action.
Do not let visible trade-offs crowd out hidden low-probability high-impact failure paths.
Do not treat the plan as safe merely because each conjunctive success step looks likely; a disjunctive failure path can still dominate.
affordances.name: Thresholded downside governance before commitment

dropped_material: Did not extract a separate risk-reward bet-selection affordance; that belongs more cleanly to calculated-risk-taking.
Did not extract relation semantics as affordances; systems-thinking, inversion, and bias conflicts are supporting context here.
Did not promote AI persona risk-aversion or finance examples because they illustrate application settings rather than a distinct treatment requirement.
review_notes.normalization_note: Collapsed the source's worst-case, hidden-failure-path, knowns-and-unknowns, disjunctive-system, and threshold/monitoring material into one operational affordance because they serve the same downside-governance move.
open_questions: Review may later decide whether the human-element warning deserves its own affordance; it is intentionally left out to avoid broadening this record past downside governance.

source_evidence.source_quote: Risk Assessment is a fundamental mental model defined not just by calculating likelihood and impact, but by the **systematic effort to anticipate potential threats, challenges, and pitfalls** associated with a decision, action, or system.
By articulating the factors involved in a worst-case scenario, one can dedicate time to preventing those specific failures, which can be quicker and more efficient than aiming for a direct goal.
Conversely, risk evaluation in a complex system (like a nuclear reactor or the human body) involves a **disjunctive structure** (failure occurs if *any* essential component fails).
The least obvious and most dangerous risks, requiring concerted effort (like **scenario analysis** or systems thinking) to uncover.
This process inherently requires assessing risks by setting clear thresholds for success or failure
Risk assessment is strongest when surviving failure matters as much as chasing upside.
The model adds value when the team needs to surface low-probability, high-impact consequences before commitment.
It is a strong fit when plans need explicit mitigations, thresholds, and monitoring rather than generic caution language.
If decisions are reversible or low-stakes, too much time spent analyzing risk is inefficient.

The model is weakened if practitioners fall into the trap of analyzing everything equally ("Don't boil the ocean") instead of narrowing the focus to issues with the **biggest impact** and those they can actually influence.
The model fails when checklists, matrices, or warnings are produced to satisfy governance optics without changing the actual decision or safeguards.
It helps uncover **unknown unknowns** by getting a full picture of the interdependencies within the system.
**Ask: "Is the worst case bad enough?"**.

treatment_requirements.description: Identify the hidden, worst-case, and disjunctive failure paths that could dominate the outcome before treating the plan as safe.
evidence_required: The strategic goal, decision, action, or system being assessed.
The worst-case scenario factors that would make failure matter.
Low-probability high-impact consequences or disjunctive component failures that could collapse the plan.
Unknown unknowns or interdependencies that the current analysis has not yet made visible.
good_output_shape: Names the few failure paths that could dominate the decision.
Separates hidden failure paths from ordinary visible trade-offs.
Shows which system interdependencies or unknowns still need de-risking.
Keeps the treatment focused on downside governance rather than risk-reward bet selection.

treatment_requirements.description: Turn each material exposure into a mitigation, threshold, monitoring signal, or safeguard that can change the decision before commitment.
evidence_required: The mitigation attached to each material exposure.
The threshold for success, failure, escalation, or reversal.
The monitoring signal that shows whether the risk is becoming real.
The decision, safeguard, or execution change triggered by the threshold.
good_output_shape: States the mitigation and threshold next to the risk it governs.
Defines what will be monitored before and after commitment.
Explains how the safeguard changes the plan if the risk materializes.
Avoids caution language that does not alter a decision or operating guardrail.

treatment_requirements.description: Prioritize the biggest-impact and influenceable fragility drivers instead of spreading analysis evenly across every imaginable risk.
evidence_required: The candidate risks or fragility drivers under review.
Impact evidence showing which risks could actually dominate the outcome.
Influence evidence showing which risks the team can prevent, mitigate, monitor, or escalate.
Risks deliberately set aside because they are low-impact, uninfluenceable, reversible, or already covered.
good_output_shape: Explains why the selected risk drivers deserve depth.
Drops secondary variables that do not change action.
Protects action speed for reversible or low-stakes choices.
Documents set-asides without turning them into a generic risk inventory.

case_evidence_needed: The dependency chain this intervention assumes will hold.
The first threshold where delay, overload, or information-hiding would cascade into a worse second-order outcome.
The incentive that would cause a local team to protect its metric while making the system worse.
The behavior the intervention will teach surrounding teams to repeat if it works locally.
do_not_use_when: The consequence chain is not anchored in the real mechanism, actor reactions, or constraints of the case.
Complexity and downstream effects are being used to postpone commitment indefinitely instead of naming a threshold for acting under uncertainty.
use_when: A move improves an obvious local metric but may degrade incentives, trust, coordination, resilience, or future option value downstream.
The order of decisions, dependencies, or stakeholder reactions determines whether the same action helps or hurts.
Customers, competitors, partners, regulators, or teammates are likely to change behavior in response to the initial move.

diagnostic_questions: What dependency chain must hold, where can delay, overload, or information-hiding flip the result, and what incentive would make a team protect its metric while worsening the system?
If the intervention works locally, what behavior will surrounding teams learn to repeat, and how could that adaptation affect the next cycle?
affordances.mechanism: Before endorsing an attractive local gain, map the dependency chain, first reversal threshold, and incentive or behavioral adaptation that could turn the immediate win into later system damage.
misuse_guards: Do not invent long speculative consequence chains that are not anchored in the real mechanism, actor reactions, or constraints of the case.
Do not use second-order language to postpone commitment indefinitely; name the threshold for acting under uncertainty.
affordances.name: Downstream reversal stress test

source_evidence.source_quote: Most useful when a move improves the obvious local metric but may degrade incentives, trust, coordination, resilience, or future option value downstream.
Works when the order of decisions, dependencies, or stakeholder reactions determines whether the same action helps or hurts.
Best when customers, competitors, partners, regulators, or teammates are likely to change behavior in response to the initial move.
The plan is approved because the immediate gain is visible, while the real outcome is created later by overloaded dependencies, delayed reactions, or local teams protecting their own metrics.

Map the dependency chain this intervention assumes will hold, identify the first threshold where delay, overload, or information-hiding would cascade into a worse second-order outcome, and name the incentive that would cause a local team to protect its metric while making the system worse.
If this intervention works locally, what behavior will it teach the surrounding teams to repeat, and how could that adaptation make the next cycle slower, noisier, or harder to govern?
Danger when the model is used to invent long speculative chains that feel sophisticated but are not anchored in the real mechanism, actor reactions, or constraints of the case.
A major anti-pattern is invoking complexity and downstream effects as a reason to postpone commitment indefinitely instead of naming the threshold for acting under uncertainty.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to identify the downstream dependency chain, first reversal threshold, and local incentive before endorsing the intervention.
evidence_required: The visible first-order gain being considered.
The downstream dependency that must hold for the gain to stay beneficial.
The first delay, overload, or information-hiding threshold that would flip the outcome.
The local metric or incentive that could make a team protect itself while worsening the system.
good_output_shape: States the immediate gain and the dependency chain it assumes.
Names the first cascade threshold where the result could become worse.
Identifies the incentive that could protect a local metric while damaging the broader system.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to check what surrounding teams will learn to repeat if the local intervention succeeds.
evidence_required: The team, partner, supplier, customer, or other actor that will adapt around the intervention.
The repeated behavior the local relief teaches or rewards.
How that adaptation could make the next cycle slower, noisier, or harder to govern.
good_output_shape: Describes the local relief the intervention creates.
Names the behavior surrounding actors are likely to repeat.
Explains whether that adaptation makes the next cycle slower, noisier, or harder to govern.

case_evidence_needed: The time, money, emotional effort, identity, or other investment that is already irretrievable.
The next unit of cost, effort, exposure, or commitment being requested.
Expected future returns, marginal benefit, cash-flow reality, or comparable alternative use of resources.
Disconfirming evidence, exit criteria, or performance thresholds that would stop or change the next phase.
do_not_use_when: The exit argument ignores real remaining upside, learning value, switching costs, or residual option value.
Continuation is justified by attractive expected value after current facts and future costs and benefits are separated from past expenditure.
There is no prior-investment pressure and the live problem is ordinary option comparison or forecasting.
use_when: Prior time, money, identity, or effort is making an exit, kill, or reallocation decision feel harder than the future economics justify.
A team keeps defending continuation because of what has already been sacrificed instead of because the next step has attractive expected value.
A project, investment, partnership, or personal commitment needs a start-again-today reset from current facts.

diagnostic_questions: If we hadn't spent any money/time on this yet, would we invest in this project today based solely on the expected future returns?
What specific performance criteria must be met to justify the next phase of investment?
How would someone entirely new to this situation advise us to proceed?
What evidence exists that would fundamentally prove our original hypothesis wrong?
Is the rationale for continuing based on past effort or on rational future financial projections?
If we quit now, is the cost truly a loss, or is it freeing up resources for a greater gain?
affordances.mechanism: When prior investment, identity, or loss discomfort makes continuation feel obligatory, remove irretrievable costs from the choice, compare only marginal future value against alternatives, and recommit only if current evidence would justify starting again today.
misuse_guards: Do not use anti-sunk-cost logic as cover for quitting without evaluating learning value, switching costs, or real future upside.
Do not let ego, blind spots, or admission-of-failure framing substitute for future viability analysis.
Do not rely on book values or historical outlays when cash-flow reality is the relevant economic evidence.
Do not continue merely because prior investment is being used to justify continued exposure.
affordances.name: Future-value recommitment before continuation

dropped_material: Did not split NPV, book values, stock-sale examples, supplier switching, or venture funding into separate domain affordances because they support the same future-value recommitment operation.

Did not promote relation curation for first-principles-thinking, optionality, systems-thinking, commitment-bias, or confirmation-bias into separate affordances; those relationships clarify this record but belong in relation semantics until reviewed otherwise.
review_notes.normalization_note: Condensed the raw source and reviewed curation into one operational contract: isolate irretrievable investment, judge the next phase by future value, and guard both against escalation and careless exit.
open_questions: Future review could decide whether exit criteria should become its own affordance if runtime traces show it changes behavior independently of the recommitment test.

source_evidence.source_quote: Rational economic theory dictates that only future costs and benefits (marginal analysis) should influence a decision, yet people often "pay attention to sunk costs".
This technique focuses exclusively on the costs and benefits of the next unit of action, making the actual amount already spent irrelevant to the current decision.

Using NPV and discounted cash flow analysis focuses on the expected future value generation of the project, providing an objective financial metric that strips away emotional attachment to past outlays.
one should focus on the economic reality of **cash flows** rather than relying on accounting book values, which often include irrelevant historical costs.
The model is strongest when time, money, identity, or effort already spent is making an exit, kill, or reallocation decision feel harder than the future economics justify.
SCF has high leverage when teams keep defending continuation because of what has already been sacrificed rather than because the next step has attractive expected value.
The model becomes especially useful when decision-makers need to reset from current facts and ask whether they would willingly recommit from zero.
The model is also misapplied when people use “don’t honor sunk costs” as cover for quitting without evaluating learning value, switching costs, or the real future upside that still remains.

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: Proactively define specific conditions (time, budget, performance metrics) under which the commitment will be stopped or changed *before* starting the project.

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: Consult individuals (friends, colleagues, consultants) who lack emotional attachment or prior history with the investment.

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: Rely on rigorous analytical tools like **marginal analysis** and focusing on **cash flows** to divorce the decision from emotional accounting.

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: "If we hadn't spent any money/time on this yet, would we invest in this project today based solely on the expected future returns?"

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: "What specific performance criteria must be met to justify the next phase of investment?"

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: "How would someone entirely new to this situation advise us to proceed?"

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: "What evidence exists that would fundamentally prove our original hypothesis wrong?"

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: "Is the rationale for continuing based on past effort or on rational future financial projections?"

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: "If we quit now, is the cost truly a loss, or is it freeing up resources for a greater gain?"
prior investment is used to justify continued exposure

treatment_requirements.description: Exclude already-incurred costs from the decision case and restate the choice as whether to continue from current facts, marginal future value, and alternative uses of the same resources.
evidence_required: The specific sunk investment that is being treated as a reason to continue.
The forward costs and benefits of the next action or phase.
The alternative investment, supplier, project, or resource use being compared against continuation.
good_output_shape: Names the sunk cost and explicitly sets it aside as irretrievable.
Answers whether the decision-maker would start or fund the commitment again today.
Recommends continue, exit, change, or reallocate based on future value rather than past expenditure.

treatment_requirements.description: Before recommending another phase, require performance criteria or falsifying evidence and distinguish real remaining upside from the psychological relief of not admitting a loss.
evidence_required: The criteria that must be met to justify the next phase of investment.
Evidence that would prove the original hypothesis wrong or show that future upside still remains.
Any real learning value, switching cost, cash-flow advantage, or residual option value that could justify continuation.
good_output_shape: States the threshold or evidence that would stop, change, or justify the next phase.
Uses outside or objective perspectives when attachment to the prior investment may be distorting judgment.
Checks remaining upside, learning value, and switching costs before treating exit as the obvious answer.

case_evidence_needed: The recurring failure pattern the rewrite is meant to solve.
Current ownership boundaries, decision rights, feedback loops, and cross-team accountability.
Evidence about whether the pattern persists once those social-system factors are made explicit.
do_not_use_when: The failure is already traced to a specific technical defect and does not depend on ownership, decision rights, or feedback design.
There is no recurring failure pattern to distinguish from the immediate technical symptom.
use_when: A team wants a full architecture rewrite or technical redesign in response to recurring failures.
Architecture debt is being used as the explanation while coordination debt or ownership ambiguity may be driving the pattern.
A cleaner technical stack could reproduce the same failure because social-system design remains unresolved.

diagnostic_questions: Are the failures caused by the technical system itself or by the social structure operating it?
Does the failure pattern persist once ownership boundaries, decision rights, and feedback loops are explicit?
Would a cleaner stack reproduce the same failure if cross-team accountability stays unresolved?
affordances.mechanism: Before approving a rewrite or redesign, test whether recurring failures persist after ownership boundaries, decision rights, feedback loops, and cross-team accountability are made explicit.
misuse_guards: Do not let architecture debt become the default explanation for coordination debt or ownership ambiguity.
Do not approve a rewrite that solves messy code while leaving decision rights, feedback loops, and accountability unresolved.
affordances.name: Test social-system causes before architecture rewrites

source_evidence.source_quote: Best when a team wants a full architecture rewrite or technical redesign, but the recurring failures may be driven by ownership ambiguity, weak feedback loops, or coordination breakdown rather than technical debt alone.
Architecture debt is used as the explanation when the recurring failures are actually coordination debt or ownership ambiguity.
A cleaner stack can reproduce the same failure if decision rights, feedback loops, or cross-team accountability remain unresolved.
The rewrite solves the symptom (messy code) while leaving the cause (unclear who decides, who owns, who has skin in the outcome) intact.
Before approving a rewrite, test whether the failures are caused by the system itself or by the social structure operating it.
Before approving a redesign, ask whether the failure pattern persists once ownership boundaries, decision rights, and feedback loops are made explicit.

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must test the social structure operating the system before treating architecture debt as the root cause of recurring failures.
evidence_required: The proposed rewrite or redesign and the failure pattern it targets.
Evidence for or against coordination debt, ownership ambiguity, weak feedback loops, or cross-team accountability gaps.
A check of whether clearer coordination would remove the failure pattern before architecture changes are approved.
good_output_shape: Separate the messy-code symptom from the coordination or ownership cause hypothesis.
Ask whether the failure pattern persists once decision rights and feedback loops are explicit.
Approve architecture work only after the social-system test does not explain the recurring failure.

case_evidence_needed: The actors, incentives, constraints, and feedback loops that interact in the case.
Any known delays between an intervention and its visible effects.
Likely downstream effects created by acting at one point in the system.
do_not_use_when: The problem is dominated by a direct, proximate cause that does not require delayed or circular system explanation.
The team is using a linear reactive frame for a genuinely circular problem without evidence of the relevant loops.
use_when: Cause and effect appear delayed, circular, or distributed across multiple actors and constraints.
No single proximate cause explains the observed pattern on its own.
A decision is likely to ramify through distant parts of the system.

diagnostic_questions: Which feedback loops, delays, or distributed interactions make a single proximate cause insufficient?
How could a decision at one point ramify into an effect at a distant one?
What parts of the system are mutually dependent rather than separable?
affordances.mechanism: When cause and effect are delayed, circular, or distributed, require a dynamic map of feedback loops, actors, constraints, and downstream effects before choosing the intervention.
misuse_guards: Do not apply reactive linear thinking to a circular interconnected system.
Do not choose a fix before the relevant feedback loops and delayed effects have been made visible.
affordances.name: Map circular feedback before selecting a fix

source_evidence.source_quote: Systems are inherently circular and proactive, not linear and reactive.
Systems dynamics often involves understanding *reinforcing feedback* (driving growth or decrease) and *balancing feedback*.
Systems thinkers utilize tools like **causal loop diagrams** and **stock and flow diagrams** to visualize the system.
Causal loop diagrams specifically showcase the feedback loops at work.
Works when multiple actors, incentives, constraints, and feedback loops interact so that no single proximate cause explains the pattern on its own.
It allows leaders to visualize the dynamics of complex systems and **foresee how a decision at one point will ramify to create an effect at a distant one**.
Applying a reactive, linear mode of thinking to a problem that exists in a **circular, interconnected system**.

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must represent the system dynamically, identifying relevant feedback loops and delays before recommending a fix or narrowing to a simpler analytic model.
evidence_required: Named actors or parts that mutually influence one another.
At least one suspected reinforcing or balancing feedback loop.
A downstream effect or delay that could change the outcome after the intervention.
good_output_shape: Describe the causal loop or stock-and-flow relationship in plain language.
Identify where the loop reinforces, balances, delays, or distributes the effect.
Explain how acting at one point could create effects elsewhere in the system.

case_evidence_needed: The goal the system is meant to serve.
The process or machine that produces the current outcome.
Candidate measurement points, levers, and signals of progress.
do_not_use_when: The team cannot name a variable to move, a place to intervene, or a progress signal after mapping the system.
The work is only broad explanation and cannot yet bridge from analysis into action.
use_when: A system map or causal story exists but the team still needs a concrete intervention variable.
Strategic metrics must be designed from the goal and process rather than copied from visible symptoms.
The discussion risks producing rich diagrams without saying where to intervene or what signal would show progress.

diagnostic_questions: Which variable should move, where should the intervention occur, and what signal would show progress?
What goal and process stage make this metric meaningful rather than decorative?
Which lever tied to the metric can actually adjust the process and change outcomes?
affordances.mechanism: A system explanation earns weight only when it links the goal, process, measurement point, lever, and progress signal so the map can change outcomes instead of remaining descriptive.
misuse_guards: Do not let a system map remain a rich diagram without a leverage point or progress signal.
Do not mistake understanding for implementation when the source says systems thinking cannot bridge that gap by itself.
affordances.name: Convert system maps into measured leverage points

source_evidence.source_quote: This involves first knowing the goal, then understanding the process (the "machine"), identifying the key points in the process that are best for measurement, and finally, exploring how to create levers (tied to those metrics) to adjust the process and change outcomes.
These metrics are often constructed alongside **process flow diagrams** and procedures manuals.
Danger when teams produce rich diagrams, broad causal stories, or elegant architecture language but still cannot say which variable to move, where to intervene, or what signal would show progress.
It provides understanding but **cannot bridge the gap to implementation**; that requires human spirit and resolve.

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must turn the system map into a goal-process-measure-lever chain, then identify what signal should move if the intervention is working.
evidence_required: A named goal for the system.
A process view showing where the current outcome is produced.
At least one candidate metric tied to a lever that can adjust the process.
good_output_shape: State the goal before choosing the metric.
Locate the measurement point within the process or machine.
Name the lever, the variable it should move, and the progress signal to watch.

case_evidence_needed: The immediate case details and the repeated event pattern that makes a local patch suspect.
Candidate structures, designs, or rules that could have produced the observed outcome.
The mental model or assumption currently being used to explain the system.
do_not_use_when: The case is a bounded one-off event with no recurring pattern, structural signal, or system behavior to diagnose.
The available evidence does not yet identify any system design, assumption, or repeated outcome to examine.
use_when: Recurring symptoms, repeated escalations, or oscillating outcomes suggest the local patch is missing the source behavior.
A visible event may be a symptom of deeper structural flaws rather than the true problem.
A leadership or management discussion is staying on the immediate case and needs the machine-level cause.

diagnostic_questions: What structure, design, or recurring pattern produced this visible event?
What changes when the discussion moves from the case-at-hand level to the machine level?
Which assumption about the system needs to be exposed before others can check it?
affordances.mechanism: Before treating a visible event, separate the immediate case from the system design that produced it, expose the working assumptions, and decide whether recurring symptoms point to deeper structure rather than a local fix.
misuse_guards: Do not treat the surface event as the root cause when the source points to deeper structural flaws.
Do not let attention to the immediate case become micromanagement while the underlying system remains unexamined.
affordances.name: Diagnose recurring events through system structure

source_evidence.source_quote: The presenting issue (the "event") is often symptomatic of deeper problems or structural flaws within the system.
By looking deeper, systems thinkers seek to understand **root causes** rather than merely treating surface symptoms.
A key practice is to **expose mental models to the light of day**.

discussions should be conducted at two levels: the **machine level** (exploring the structure and design that produced the outcome) and the **case-at-hand level** (addressing the immediate situation).
Focusing only on the "case-at-hand" leads to micromanagement, whereas systems thinking requires addressing the underlying system.
Most useful when recurring symptoms, repeated escalations, or oscillating outcomes suggest the local patch is not touching the real source of behavior.

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must handle both the case-at-hand level and the machine level, naming what immediate response is needed and what underlying structure should be investigated or redesigned.
evidence_required: A visible symptom, event, escalation, or oscillating outcome.
A candidate machine-level structure or design that plausibly produced the symptom.
An exposed assumption about why the system is behaving this way.
good_output_shape: Separate the immediate case response from the underlying system diagnosis.
Name the structural cause to investigate before prescribing a local fix.
State which assumption about the system should be made visible and checked.

case_evidence_needed: The named binding constraint, the dependency it controls, and the governing throughput, cycle-time, capacity, budget, or approval-latency metric.
The current quantified cap plus a forecast of which proposed action should move that cap first.
Evidence that visible local improvements would still leave system throughput capped if the named constraint is not relieved.
do_not_use_when: The limiting factor is moving every cycle, so one fixed bottleneck frame would be brittle without uncertainty framing.
The team cannot name the governing metric, current cap, and evidence that an intervention moved the cap.
use_when: A sequential flow has one queue, approval gate, specialist lane, production boundary, or other stage that clearly limits output.
Competing interventions can be ranked by how fast they increase a named bottleneck's quantified constrained capacity.
Local gain metrics or visible execution wins are masking a shared bottleneck that governs the global outcome.

diagnostic_questions: Before committing: what is the single measured limit that would still cap throughput if every local improvement in this plan succeeded next week?
If the visible pain point were improved, what dependency, queue, handoff, or approval step would still cap the system?
affordances.mechanism: Before ranking fixes, the model makes the binding limit and its governing metric the unit of action, so non-bottleneck improvements are subordinated to the cap that controls throughput.
misuse_guards: Do not optimize the step with the most noise or local reporting detail until it is proven to be the binding constraint.
Do not accept bottleneck language without the governing metric, current cap, and movement evidence.
affordances.name: Measured constraint first intervention

source_evidence.source_quote: The fundamental essence of this mental model is the recognition that in any complex system, performance is ultimately limited by **one key restricting element**, or constraint.
performance is constrained by a measurable, repeatable cap and the route to improvement requires identifying and relieving that binding limit first.

Use when success requires passing several sequential steps, but one stage clearly limits output (for example, one queue, one approval gate, one specialist bottleneck, or one production boundary), so improving non-binding stages does not raise throughput.

Use when there are competing action options, and you can name the constraint as a named bottleneck with a quantified limit (cycle-time threshold, capacity, budget floor, legal deadline, or cognitive handoff load), then rank options by how fast they increase this constrained capacity.
Use when departments, teams, or models propose fixes that optimize their own KPIs while ignoring the shared bottleneck
Danger when the limiting factor is moving every cycle because the market, counterparty, or objective is changing too quickly for single-constraint optimization to remain stable.

Before committing: what is the single measured limit that would still cap throughput if every local improvement in this plan succeeded next week? If you cannot name the binding constraint, the dependency it controls, and the metric that would move first, you are not improving the system -- you are improving a visible step.
Teams optimize the step with the most noise, pain, or local reporting detail while the real throughput ceiling sits in a different dependency, handoff, or approval step.
without naming the governing metric, the current cap, and the evidence that the cap actually moved after intervention.

treatment_requirements.description: Require each intervention to forecast movement on the measured binding limit before visible non-bottleneck improvements are accepted.
evidence_required: Named bottleneck with quantified limit and current baseline metric.
Explanation of why proposed non-bottleneck work would or would not raise system throughput.
good_output_shape: A constraint-first sequence that names the cap, ranks options by expected cap movement, and defers local improvements that leave the cap unchanged.
A measurable first-movement signal tied to the dependency controlled by the binding constraint.

case_evidence_needed: Post-change evidence showing whether the original bottleneck moved and which queue, dependency, or approval step now caps throughput.
A checkpoint after each major change for re-scoring bottleneck hypotheses.
Evidence about scarce team, approval, budget, or specialist time that may be diverted away from the current constraint.
do_not_use_when: There is no measurable bottleneck movement or intervention cycle to retest.
The issue is only constraint identification and the situation-specific execution path has not been designed yet.
use_when: A first bottleneck has been partially relieved and the plan is still funding the same intervention package.
Order of operations matters because upstream prerequisites or handoffs determine whether downstream work can have incremental impact.
Parallel work may be consuming scarce team, approval, or specialist capacity needed to relieve the binding limit.

diagnostic_questions: If this first bottleneck actually moves, what step becomes the next cap within two weeks, and what evidence in this plan would tell us the constraint has already shifted there?
Which task in this plan is consuming the same scarce team, approval slot, or specialist time needed at the bottleneck?
affordances.mechanism: After a bottleneck is relieved, the model requires a fresh constraint check because the next limiting step may control throughput and old interventions can become misdirected.
misuse_guards: Do not keep investing in the initially identified bottleneck without reassessing whether it is still binding.
Do not sequence downstream symptom work while upstream prerequisites remain unresolved.
affordances.name: Retest shifted constraints before continuing

source_evidence.source_quote: Constraint shift without retest
The initial bottleneck is removed, but teams continue the same interventions because they do not measure whether a new bottleneck has moved into a different step.
**Constraint cadence:** Re-score hypotheses against the measured bottleneck after each major change and repoint action sequencing when the constraint moves.
If this first bottleneck actually moves, what step becomes the next cap within two weeks, and what evidence in this plan would tell us the constraint has already shifted there?
Use when order of operations matters and a prerequisite constraint has to be relieved before parallel work can have incremental impact.
Work is sequenced on a downstream symptom while upstream prerequisites are unresolved, creating rework, coordination drag, and temporary gains that disappear after the next handoff.

**theory-of-constraints vs commitment-bias:** theory-of-constraints conflicts with commitment-bias (commitment bias) when the team over-invests in improving the initially identified bottleneck rather than reassessing whether that constraint is still the binding one.
Identifying the constraint (the *what*) does not automatically solve the problem of *how* to execute the solution.

treatment_requirements.description: Re-score bottleneck hypotheses after each major change and update action sequencing when the binding constraint moves.
evidence_required: Baseline and current measurement for the original bottleneck.
Candidate next cap and the evidence that would show the constraint has shifted there.
good_output_shape: A revised sequence that stops funding stale bottleneck fixes and points the next action at the newly measured cap.
A short retest cadence that names the next cap, the movement signal, and the work to stop if it competes for scarce constraint-relief capacity.

case_evidence_needed: The scarce resource and competing objectives that cannot all be pursued.
The option, task, customer preference, benefit, or project that would receive less time, money, attention, or priority.
The hidden opportunity cost or displaced benefit attached to saying yes.
The assumptions that must be true for the chosen sacrifice to be acceptable.
do_not_use_when: The situation has no material scarcity, conflicting goals, displaced benefit, or allocation decision.
The team only wants to acknowledge tension rhetorically while keeping every option and priority intact.
The problem is a simple preference list and lacks enough evidence to weigh material costs and benefits.
use_when: Pursuing one objective would weaken another and the real decision is what to sacrifice, delay, or downweight.
Time, attention, money, or political capital make doing everything impossible and priorities must be ranked.
The recommendation hides opportunity cost, displaced benefit, or the actual resource reallocation behind balance language.

diagnostic_questions: Which good thing are we choosing to sacrifice, delay, or downweight?
What hidden cost of saying yes, opportunity cost, or displaced benefit is being ignored?
What would you have to believe for this trade-off to be acceptable?
If no resources, options, or priorities change, is this only trade-off talk?
affordances.mechanism: When scarce resources make desirable options conflict, force the recommendation to name what is sacrificed, delayed, downweighted, or killed, expose the hidden cost of saying yes, and stress-test the assumptions that make the chosen trade-off acceptable.
misuse_guards: Do not let trade-off language substitute for actual reallocation, option killing, or priority ranking.
Do not use a simple pro-con list when material costs and benefits need systematic weighing.
Do not let comprehensiveness theater turn the work into gathering everything after a bounded hypothesis test is enough.
Do not let hidden assumptions, defaults, or legacy priorities preserve the current allocation after new evidence.
affordances.name: Allocation-backed sacrifice before recommendation

dropped_material: Did not extract a generic balance affordance; the source-backed operational surface is narrower than the model name.
Did not split communication compression, Southwest positioning, management focus, and learning accuracy into separate affordances; they illustrate the same sacrifice-and-allocation mechanism.
Did not promote relation curation or structured tensions into runtime behavior.

source_evidence.source_quote: The fundamental essence of Calculated Risk Taking is the process of making high-stakes decisions by **estimating the parameters of uncertainty** and systematically evaluating potential outcomes rather than relying on mere intuition or default behavior.

Acknowledging that all decisions are fundamentally "bets" based on our current beliefs, it requires getting comfortable with uncertainty and redefining "right" and "wrong" based on the quality of the decision process, not just the outcome.
Calculated risk taking is strongest when downside, upside, and assumptions can be pressure-tested well enough to improve the bet.
The model adds value when the right move is neither reckless action nor timid avoidance, but a disciplined wager with bounded downside.
It is a strong fit when waiting for certainty would itself create material risk or lost opportunity.
This technique involves spelling out all the implicit assumptions underlying a specific perspective or proposed growth strategy.
performing a sensitivity analysis helps to test the stability of the conclusion by running it across different input variables.
"Is the worst case bad enough?"
The model fails when teams call a move calculated even though assumptions were weak, downside was not bounded, or dissenting evidence was ignored.
This occurs when decision-makers fail to recognize the point of sufficient analysis and get stuck in endless "what-ifs".

source_evidence.source_quote: Circle of Control is a decision model for separating what you can directly influence from what you cannot control.

In practice, start by listing the situation facts, then split them into three buckets: direct control, indirect influence, and no control. Create actions only for the first two buckets and monitoring rules for the third. This prevents reactive planning loops.
Teams can immediately identify controllable levers such as communication cadence, pricing experiments, staffing, sequencing, and risk limits.
Works when the next step depends on separating what one team owns directly, what it can influence through escalation or negotiation, and what must be monitored rather than solved immediately.
Best when the organization needs to move under uncertainty without pretending that every important variable can be controlled first.
A common anti-pattern is labeling difficult-but-influenceable factors as "outside control" too early, which can become avoidance disguised as realism.
Resolution requires periodic reclassification: what was uncontrollable yesterday may become influenceable after new information or changed incentives.
False helplessness happens when teams classify influenceable variables as uncontrollable.
Control illusion happens when teams overestimate intervention power in adaptive systems.
Rigid categorization happens when the buckets are never revisited.
Teams optimize what is immediately controllable while missing how broader feedback loops will undo the local gain.
Mitigations include a recurring review cadence, explicit evidence for each classification decision, and pre-mortem checks.

Useful pre-mortem questions: "Which item did we mark uncontrollable without evidence?" "Where are we assuming control we do not actually have?" "What one action would increase our influence on the highest-impact external factor?"

treatment_requirements.description: List the facts first, classify them into direct-control, indirect-influence, and no-control buckets, then generate actions only where intervention or influence is possible.
evidence_required: The concrete situation facts before classification.

good_output_shape: A control map that separates owned levers, influenceable conditions, and monitored external variables.
Actions attached to direct-control and indirect-influence items rather than to no-control conditions.
Monitoring rules for uncontrollable items instead of reactive planning loops.

evidence_required: The evidence supporting each direct-control, indirect-influence, or no-control classification.

New information, incentive changes, or stakeholder position changes that could expand or shrink influence.
good_output_shape: Flags any item marked uncontrollable without evidence.
States where control may be overestimated in an adaptive system.
Names a review cadence or trigger for moving items between buckets.

review_notes.normalization_note: Read the full packet, raw source, and Wave 1-3 curation. Extracted one source-backed affordance and kept the record dormant for review rather than runtime promotion.
