source_evidence.source_quote: In practice, start by listing the situation facts, then split them into three buckets: direct control, indirect influence, and no control. Create actions only for the first two buckets and monitoring rules for the third. This prevents reactive planning loops.
Resolution requires periodic reclassification: what was uncontrollable yesterday may become influenceable after new information or changed incentives.
Mitigations include a recurring review cadence, explicit evidence for each classification decision, and pre-mortem checks.
A common anti-pattern is labeling difficult-but-influenceable factors as "outside control" too early, which can become avoidance disguised as realism.

absence_records.reason: The source gives concrete multi-agent AI simulation examples, but it does not provide a general treatment requirement for when to build digital twins beyond the broader adaptive-system mapping and foresight material.

source_evidence.source_quote: The CAS model is applied by creating AI agents as **"digital twin creators"** to clone countless realistic personas and scenarios.
Advanced applications include simulating an entire buying committee or social network using multi-agent systems.

absence_records.reason: The latticework passages support using multiple models around CAS, but in this record they are better treated as review context and relation material rather than a separate operational affordance.

source_evidence.source_quote: This model suggests that because multiple factors shape almost every system, understanding requires tools from varied disciplines (like psychology, mathematics, engineering, and economics).

case_evidence_needed: The adapting agents or components and how they respond to one another.
The feedback loops, delays, incentives, boundaries, or limiting factors that can reshape the intervention.
A candidate leverage point and the learning, coordination, or adaptation signal it is expected to move.
do_not_use_when: The case is a bounded static-machine problem with no evidence of adapting agents, feedback, delays, or incentives.
Complexity language is being used to avoid naming a concrete leverage point, experiment, or owner for improvement.
use_when: People, teams, markets, or components are continuously adapting to each other rather than behaving like fixed parts.
Feedback, delays, or incentives keep reshaping the environment after each intervention.
Improving the whole depends on finding a small change that alters learning loops, coordination, or adaptation.

diagnostic_questions: Which agents are adapting to one another, and how will they respond after the intervention?
Which feedback loop, delay, incentive, boundary, or limiting factor could reshape the result over time?
Where can a small change alter learning loops, coordination, or adaptation instead of micromanaging parts?
affordances.mechanism: When many agents respond to one another, an intervention changes the system that will later receive the next intervention, so the answer must map agents, feedback, delays, incentives, and the small leverage point before prescribing action.
misuse_guards: Do not let complexity language become an excuse for failing to name leverage points, experiments, or ownership.
Do not micromanage isolated parts when the whole depends on learning loops, coordination, or adaptation.
Do not ignore delays, limiting factors, or false boundaries when designing the intervention.
affordances.name: Choose leverage points inside adaptive feedback

source_evidence.source_quote: CAS is strongest when people, teams, markets, or components are continuously responding to each other rather than behaving like fixed parts in a static machine.
The model has high leverage when the intervention itself changes the system and the system then changes how future interventions will work.
CAS is especially useful when improving the whole depends on understanding where a small change can alter learning loops, coordination, or adaptation.
The model is distorted when complexity language is used to excuse the absence of concrete leverage points, experiments, or ownership for improvement.
Mistreating a system by failing to account for delays, limiting factors, and false boundaries can lead to misdesign and poor outcomes.

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must identify the adapting agents, feedback or delay structure, and a concrete leverage point before recommending what to change.
evidence_required: Named agents or components that are adapting in response to one another.
At least one feedback loop, delay, incentive, boundary, or limiting factor that changes how action will play out.
A specific leverage point, owner, or experiment tied to a learning, coordination, or adaptation signal.
good_output_shape: Name the agents and the response pattern that makes the situation adaptive.
Explain how feedback, delay, incentive, or boundary conditions will reshape the intervention over time.
Recommend the small leverage point to try next and the signal that should change if it is working.

case_evidence_needed: The ambitious goal or desired outcome the system is meant to move toward.
The current problem signal and the root-cause diagnosis, separated from the proposed plan.
The design to try, the doing required to push it through, and the result signal that will feed the next loop.
do_not_use_when: The task is already a simple one-step execution problem with no adaptive learning loop to manage.
The work cannot yet name the goal, problem, diagnosis, plan, or doing step that should be handled next.
use_when: The team has a complex adaptive problem and needs to evolve quickly through a repeated learning process.
People are jumping to plans before goals, problems, or root causes have been made explicit.
The system diagnosis is not yet connected to the doing required to produce results.

diagnostic_questions: Which step of the adaptive loop are we actually in: goal, problem, root-cause diagnosis, design, or doing?
What root cause must be diagnosed before this plan can be treated as more than a guess?
What has to be done to push the design through to results, and what signal will feed the next loop?
affordances.mechanism: Good use keeps complex-system work in a fast recurring sequence of goal, problem, diagnosis, plan, and execution, because understanding a system is not the same as pushing a design through emergent implementation.
misuse_guards: Do not let system understanding masquerade as implementation.
Do not skip the ordered sequence when the source says the steps must be done one at a time and in order.
Do not assume a blueprint will absorb the implementation complexity of an adaptive system.
affordances.name: Run the ordered adaptive learning loop

source_evidence.source_quote: Successful engagement with adaptive systems depends on a continuous, looping process, done fast and continuously to evolve quickly. The steps must be done one at a time and in order to avoid suboptimal outcomes:
Accurately diagnosing root causes.
Doing what's necessary to push these designs through to results.
Systems thinking can identify solutions and pinpoint problems, but it often cannot bridge the gap between *understanding* and *implementation*.
Implementation is more complex and "more emergent than amenable to a blueprint".

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must place the case in the ordered adaptive loop and state the next disciplined step instead of treating system understanding as sufficient implementation.
evidence_required: A stated goal or desired outcome.
A problem statement and root-cause diagnosis that precede the design recommendation.
A plan, execution step, and result signal that will let the system learn from what happens next.
good_output_shape: Separate goals, problems, root causes, plans, and doing instead of collapsing them into one recommendation.
Name which step is current and which step would be premature to skip toward.
Close with the execution move and feedback signal needed to continue the adaptive loop.

case_evidence_needed: The framework, system map, or perspective currently being used to explain the case.
The implicit assumptions that must be true for that explanation to work.
Disconfirming data, outside perspectives, or bias checks that could challenge the favored conclusion.
do_not_use_when: No system framework, strategic perspective, or assumption set is carrying the recommendation.
The needed assumptions and disconfirming evidence have already been made explicit enough for review.
use_when: A framework, system map, or strategic perspective depends on implicit assumptions.
An expert or team may be substituting a familiar problem type for a genuinely novel context.
The evidence could be filtered by motivated reasoning, confirmation bias, or model-defending behavior.

diagnostic_questions: What are the assumptions embedded in the framework being used here?
What would have to be true for this scenario or recommendation to work?
Are we pursuing this conclusion because the evidence supports it, or because we want it to be true?
affordances.mechanism: Because adaptive systems require disconfirming feedback to learn, a system map earns weight only after the answer makes its assumptions explicit, tests what would have to be true, and checks whether a familiar or desired conclusion is defending itself.
misuse_guards: Do not assume a familiar framework fits a genuinely novel adaptive-system context.
Do not let motivated reasoning or confirmation bias filter out the feedback needed for adaptation.
Do not rely on a system map whose assumptions and underlying logic have not been made visible.
affordances.name: Expose assumptions before trusting the system map

source_evidence.source_quote: Strategy demands making assumptions and underlying logic visible and rigorous.
A key heuristic is to use the **"What would you have to believe?"** question to spell out all the implicit assumptions in a perspective, forcing clarity on the systemic foundations of a viewpoint.
Experienced problem solvers risk assuming **"I have seen this one before, it is an X problem"**.
When analyzing systemic behavior, there is a risk that people will process information to reach a conclusion they *want* rather than discovering what is true.
Confirmation bias (model-defending behavior) actively fights the accumulation of new, disconfirming data necessary for system adaptation.
What are the assumptions embedded in the framework I am using? What would have to be true for this scenario to work?.
Am I pursuing an outcome because I *want* it to be true (motivated reasoning), or because the evidence supports it?.

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must spell out the assumptions carrying the system view and test them against disconfirming evidence before relying on the map.
evidence_required: The current framework, perspective, or system map.
The assumptions that must hold for the view to explain observed behavior.
Evidence or outside perspective that could disconfirm the favored interpretation.
good_output_shape: List the load-bearing assumptions in the system view.
Ask what would have to be true for the scenario or recommendation to work.
Name the disconfirming evidence or opposing perspective that would prevent model-defending behavior.

Succeeding in this environment requires mastering increasingly complex systems and skills.
Applying a CAS mindset requires focusing on structure, embracing iteration, and leveraging modeling tools to test outcomes.

case_evidence_needed: The commitment-driving number or forecast and the resource, scope, reputation, or irreversible action it controls.
The upper and lower bound, lower-confidence bound, or largest earned forecast error that should change the move.
Evidence that would narrow or widen the range enough to change the commitment.
do_not_use_when: The conversation is only adding caveats and ranges without naming the action threshold that would still trigger a decision.
The numbers are being used to look rigorous while the evidence remains anecdotal, sparse, or regime-unstable.
use_when: A forecast, recommendation, or estimate is about to determine resources, reputation, irreversible action, headcount, spend, rollout scope, or lock-in.
A decision tree, capital-allocation spreadsheet, or branch model is carrying more certainty than its evidence base has earned.

diagnostic_questions: Which number in this recommendation is driving the commitment, and what would have to be true for that number to deserve this much trust?

What action in this plan is being sized to the center estimate rather than to the lower-confidence bound, and how far would the move shrink if we used the largest forecast error the evidence base has actually earned?
Would you be really shocked if the outcome fell outside this upper or lower bound?
affordances.mechanism: Before a forecast, recommendation, or estimate controls resources, convert confidence into an explicit range and size the action to the uncertainty the evidence has earned, not to the center forecast or upside story.
misuse_guards: Do not let calibration talk replace commitment; caveats and ranges must still end in an action threshold.
Do not treat precise percentages, confidence intervals, or branch weights as calibrated judgment when the underlying evidence is anecdotal or regime-unstable.
Do not size commitment to the upside story when the evidence has only earned a wider range.

case_evidence_needed: The metric or model output being trusted and the data-generating process behind it.
Known weaknesses in sampling, attribution, denominator quality, missing data, counterparty control, comparables, or regime stability.
The recommendation that would reverse if the apparent precision proved false.
do_not_use_when: The measurement process is already validated and the live issue is not confidence in the instrument but some other constraint.
The user only needs qualitative self-assessment rather than a decision based on a precise-looking metric or output.
use_when: A dashboard, pilot metric, model output, benchmark, or spreadsheet is being treated as decision-grade because its number is clean, frequent, or model-produced.
A confidence claim rests on thin, filtered, counterpart-controlled, or regime-shifted samples.

diagnostic_questions: Which metric is being trusted because it looks precise, and what part of the data-generating process -- sampling, attribution, missing denominator, or counterparty control -- would make that precision false enough to reverse the recommendation?

Which confidence claim in this plan is acting like measurement even though it comes from a thin, filtered, or regime-shifted sample, and what evidence would widen the range enough to change the commitment?
affordances.mechanism: Treat dashboards, model outputs, pilot metrics, and other numerical displays as decision-grade only after the data-generating process has earned trust; apparent precision is not calibration unless sampling, attribution, denominators, and input control can survive scrutiny.
misuse_guards: Do not let dashboards or model outputs inherit decision-grade status from clean presentation before the data-generating process is trusted.
Do not mistake precise quantified language for calibrated judgment when samples are thin, filtered, unstable, or controlled by a counterparty.
Do not override subjective perception with an instrument unless the instrument itself is validated.
affordances.name: Instrument trust before precise-looking confidence

treatment_requirements.description: Separate numerical precision from measurement trust by naming which part of the data-generating process could make the clean number misleading enough to reverse the recommendation.
evidence_required: The precise-looking metric, dashboard value, pilot result, or model output being treated as decision-grade.
Information about sampling, attribution, missing denominators, counterparty control, or input quality.
good_output_shape: The output states whether the number is an earned instrument or only apparent precision.
The output names the measurement weakness that would change or reverse the recommendation.

treatment_requirements.description: When a confidence claim comes from a thin, filtered, or regime-shifted sample, widen the range or downgrade the commitment until the evidence base earns narrower confidence.
evidence_required: The sample, benchmark, pilot, or forecast basis behind the confidence claim.
Evidence about filtered examples, unstable regimes, thin comparables, or counterpart-controlled data.
good_output_shape: The recommendation distinguishes the stated number from the confidence the sample can actually support.
The output identifies what evidence would narrow or widen the range enough to change the commitment.

case_evidence_needed: The claim or answer, the stated numerical confidence level, and reasons for that specific level.
The method used to judge the quality of those reasons.
Any self-test, practice answer, outside view, or probing from others that checks the confidence against reality.
do_not_use_when: Repeated self-interrogation is delaying timely action after enough evidence has been gathered to choose.
The case needs commitment sizing or instrument-trust auditing more than a method check on the speaker's confidence.
use_when: A person or team asserts confidence in a claim, belief, pitch, or answer but has not exposed how that confidence was generated.
A learner, expert, or strategist may be confusing fluency, familiarity, or conviction with genuine mastery.

diagnostic_questions: On a scale of 0 to 100%, how confident are you?
What reasons do you have to hold that specific level of confidence?
What method did you use to judge the quality of those reasons?
affordances.mechanism: For any claim, elicit a numerical confidence level, the reasons for that level, and the method used to judge those reasons, then move the conversation from assertion to the quality of the confidence-producing method.
misuse_guards: Do not accept recognition fluency as mastery; make the person supply the complete and accurate answer or use a practice test.
Do not let slow-thinking calibration become analysis paralysis that prevents timely action.
Do not treat intelligence or narrative skill as a guarantee that confidence is calibrated.
affordances.name: Method-first interrogation of confidence

dropped_material: Did not turn the jazz analogy or broad humility language into affordances because they do not add sharper runtime evidence or treatment requirements.
review_notes.normalization_note: This record keeps guided metacognition operational by requiring a confidence number, reasons, and a method check, plus a concrete fluency-versus-mastery test.

source_evidence.source_quote: **Ask for a numerical measure of confidence** in the claim (e.g., "On a scale of 0 to 100%, how confident are you?").
**Ask what reasons** they have to hold that specific level of confidence.
**Ask what method** they used to judge the quality of their reasons, then focus the conversation on assessing that methodology.
An anti-pattern is looking at a quiz question and thinking, "I know that," without actually taking the time to write out the full answer, thereby failing to calibrate genuine competence.
an over-emphasis on perfect calibration can lead to **analysis paralysis**, where reflection thwarts timely action.
Smart people, having greater confidence in their beliefs, are better at generating narratives to sway themselves and others, making them more prone to **motivated reasoning**

treatment_requirements.description: Ask for the confidence number, the reasons behind it, and the method used to judge those reasons before weighing the claim.
evidence_required: The claim or belief being assessed.
The stated confidence number, supporting reasons, and confidence-judging method.
good_output_shape: The output reports confidence as a number and distinguishes reasons from the method used to evaluate those reasons.
The output shifts critique toward method quality instead of only arguing for or against the claim.

treatment_requirements.description: When confidence may reflect surface fluency, require an actual answer, self-test, practice test, or comparable reality check before treating the confidence as mastery.
evidence_required: The learning or skill claim being made.
Evidence from a complete answer, practice test, quiz, or comparable performance check.
good_output_shape: The response distinguishes familiarity from demonstrated mastery.
The response asks for or evaluates a concrete performance attempt rather than accepting recognition fluency.

dropped_material: Did not promote the pilot, target-range, or jazz analogies as standalone affordances; only the instrument analogy materially supports a distinct audit requirement.

Did not extract relation records for metacognition, probabilistic thinking, outside view, confirmation bias, authority bias, or bias blind spot because this artifact is focused on model-use affordances.
Did not add a complete calibration scoring algorithm; the source gives questions, ranges, and checks rather than a formula.
review_notes.normalization_note: Extracted three source-backed operational affordances: size commitments to earned ranges, audit apparent precision against instrument trust, and interrogate confidence by method before weighing claims.
open_questions: A reviewer may choose to split learning/mastery calibration from business-claim confidence later, but the source supports the same method-first interrogation pattern for both.

source_evidence.source_quote: **Confidence Calibration** is fundamentally the process of aligning one's subjective assessment of knowledge, skill, or prediction accuracy with objective reality.
By demanding a realistic evaluation of uncertainty, calibration motivates individuals to seek new information and improve their beliefs faster.

case_evidence_needed: The option set, including the preferences or goals each option serves.
The payoff upside, downside, and probability evidence for each serious branch.
The trigger condition, approval, counterparty response, threshold, or kill criterion that chooses between branches.
do_not_use_when: Payoffs or probabilities are too unstable, so the tree would create false precision and confidence rather than guidance.
Strong feedback loops, path dependence, or interdependencies make the branches less independent than the diagram implies.
The branch labels are too vague to become clear hypotheses that can be tested or acted on.
use_when: A decision has explicit branches, trigger conditions, or kill criteria that can be made visible before resources are committed.
Thresholds, approvals, or likely countermoves can be expressed clearly enough that the tree sharpens action rather than merely documenting options.
The next action depends on naming the branch that activates if one approval, data signal, legal condition, or counterpart response breaks the favored path.

diagnostic_questions: Which condition, approval, data signal, or counterpart response decides which branch activates next?
Which threshold flips this recommendation from test to commitment, and what branch takes over after that cutoff?
Are the branch labels clear hypotheses that can be tested, or just option names that document the discussion?
affordances.mechanism: Before choosing, the model turns options into visible branches with preferences, payoffs, probabilities, and trigger or kill conditions, so the next action follows explicit logic rather than a flat pro-con list.
misuse_guards: Do not use a decision tree as a polished pro-con list without payoffs, probabilities, and explicit branch rules.
Do not rely on a neat tree when unstable payoffs, unstable probabilities, or branch interdependencies create false precision.
Do not treat vague branch labels as analysis unless they drive testing or action.
affordances.name: Branch triggers with weighted payoffs

source_evidence.source_quote: to provide an **explicit approach to how decisions are made**, and to help decision-makers systematically evaluate options by quantifying and weighting various factors.
Decision trees integrate **preferences, payoffs, and probabilities** into a systematic decision process.

The basic pro-con method is considered "flat" because it fails to incorporate crucial information about the size of the potential payoffs (gains or losses) and the probability of those outcomes occurring.
Effective use requires explicitly defining and integrating three components for each option:
For high-stakes decisions or automated systems, the Decision Tree helps explicitly define the rules for choosing action A over action B.
**Most useful when** a decision has explicit branches, trigger conditions, or kill criteria that can be made visible before resources are committed.
**Best used when** thresholds, approvals, or likely countermoves can be expressed clearly enough that the tree sharpens action rather than merely documenting options.

Works when multiple teams can see the options, but the real decision quality depends on making explicit what happens if one approval, data signal, legal condition, or counterpart response breaks the favored path.
The effectiveness of logic trees diminishes quickly if branches contain **vague labels** instead of clear hypotheses that can be tested

treatment_requirements.description: Require each serious option to carry explicit preferences, payoff consequences, and probability estimates before comparing branches.
evidence_required: The goal or value preference behind each option.
Upside and downside payoffs for each branch.
Probability or likelihood evidence for the relevant outcomes.
good_output_shape: Shows the branch options with preferences, payoffs, and probabilities visible side by side.
Explains why the recommended branch has better supported expected value or utility than the alternatives.
Names where uncertainty remains instead of hiding it behind a neat diagram.

treatment_requirements.description: Define the action rule that makes one branch beat another, including the trigger, approval, threshold, countermove, or kill condition.
evidence_required: The condition that activates the next branch.
The branch that activates if an approval, data signal, legal condition, or counterpart response fails.
The cutoff that changes the recommendation from testing to commitment or from commitment to fallback.
good_output_shape: States the branch rule as an explicit if-this-then-that decision path.
Identifies the fallback branch when a named condition fails.
Separates actionable branch logic from vague option labels.

case_evidence_needed: The favored branch and the threshold, dependency break, or second-order consequence that could kill it.
Evidence that would show the team has crossed a reversal threshold before sunk effort locks in the wrong path.
Any shared approval, data feed, specialist, or counterpart action whose failure would collapse the branch.
do_not_use_when: There is no candidate branch or recommendation to harden yet.
The branch cannot name any threshold, dependency, evidence signal, or reroute condition that would change the action.
Feedback loops, path dependence, or interdependencies make independent branch hardening misleading.
use_when: A favored branch is being treated as decision-ready before scale, rollout, underwriting, pricing, vendor, or approval commitment.
A threshold, approval, counterparty assumption, shared dependency, or missing state of the world could reverse which branch is correct.
The branch looks precise on paper but operators still need a usable rule when the key signal is noisy, late, or contested.

diagnostic_questions: Which branch in this tree looks viable only because we have not named the threshold that kills it, the dependency that can fail first, or the second-order consequence that makes an early win a later loss?
Which threshold assumption would reverse which branch is correct, and what evidence would tell us we have already crossed it before sunk effort locks us into the wrong path?
Which branch in this tree looks precise on paper but still leaves the operator without a usable decision rule when the key signal is noisy, late, or contested?
What state of the world would force this decision onto a different branch immediately, where is that branch represented in the tree?
affordances.mechanism: After a tree appears viable, the model audits the favored branch for unstated threshold assumptions, dependency breaks, missing world states, and second-order consequences that would terminate or reroute the path before scale.
misuse_guards: Do not treat a complete-looking tree as decision-ready until branch-kill logic is named.
Do not let mean-outcome thinking hide far-from-average outcomes that would reverse the branch.
Do not let a branch survive only because a shared approval, data feed, specialist, or counterpart action remains implicit.
affordances.name: Branch-kill audit before commitment

source_evidence.source_quote: Branches are modeled neatly enough to look complete, but the tree does not name the threshold, dependency break, or second-order consequence that would terminate the favored path before scale.

Which branch in this tree looks viable only because we have not named the threshold that kills it, the dependency that can fail first, or the second-order consequence that makes an early win a later loss?
Placing excessive emphasis on the mean outcome (the base case) and insufficient weight on outcomes far from the mean.
Which threshold assumption would reverse which branch is correct, and what evidence would tell us we have already crossed it before sunk effort locks us into the wrong path?

Which branch in this tree looks precise on paper but still leaves the operator without a usable decision rule when the key signal is noisy, late, or contested, and what simpler branch instruction would they actually need at the moment of choice?

The tree compares options as if each branch can be executed independently, but a shared approval, data feed, specialist, or counterpart action can fail first and collapse the favored path before the modeled upside is reachable.

The tree is drawn around the options under discussion, but it omits the external condition that would force a different choice, making the recommendation look stable only because the decisive branch condition was never modeled.

What state of the world would force this decision onto a different branch immediately, where is that branch represented in the tree, and what evidence would tell us we are already closer to that path than the favored one?

treatment_requirements.description: Check the favored branch for the threshold, dependency break, or second-order consequence that would terminate it before scale.
evidence_required: The favored branch and its assumed success path.
The named threshold, dependency, or second-order consequence that can kill the branch.

Are we solving the right problem before diving into the efficient solution phase?
Has current ability exceeded task demands enough that boredom or static repetition is replacing stretch?
affordances.mechanism: Before treating immersion as useful, the model checks that the work has a clear goal, rapid feedback, enough skill, and a challenge just above current competence, then protects focus while keeping problem-fit and feedback visible.
misuse_guards: Do not treat a felt state of focus as evidence that the underlying problem is correct.
Do not use flow when feedback, coordination, or environmental signals are too weak to keep work reality anchored.
Do not promote automatic execution over deliberate monitoring, stopping rules, or assumption checks.
Do not force flow language onto work that lacks a clear goal, rapid feedback, or an edge-of-competence challenge.

source_evidence.source_quote: The mental model of **Flow** describes the **psychology of optimal experience**.
challenged at the peak of their ability
the strategic matching of a person’s skill level to the demands of the challenge

A defining feature of the flow state is the expectation of an immediate feedback loop. This instant knowledge of performance allows the individual to adjust their actions on the fly, reinforcing a sense of control and mastery.
Since **deep work is an activity well suited to generate a flow state**, practitioners can use structured deep thinking to trigger flow. This involves a cycle of:
**Reviewing and storing** relevant variables in working memory.
**Identifying and tackling** the specific next-step question using those variables.
**Consolidating the gains** by clearly reviewing the answer identified, then starting the process over to push to the next level of depth.
**Most useful when** the task has a clear goal, rapid feedback, and a challenge level that is high enough to demand full attention without creating panic.
**Works best when** deep work, craft practice, or complex execution depends on sustained immersion rather than fragmented multitasking.
**Best used when** a person or team already has enough skill to stretch at the edge of competence and benefit from uninterrupted concentration.
**Danger when** the team is entering flow on the wrong problem, so efficient execution outruns strategic sensemaking.
**Danger when** feedback loops, stakeholder coordination, or environmental changes are too weak for immersion to stay reality-anchored.
Regularly take a mental step back from the deep, focused work. Ensure the activity is constantly linked to a larger, overarching purpose or question.
Ensure that the foundational problem structure is correctly analyzed *before* diving into the efficient solution phase.

treatment_requirements.description: Confirm the work actually has the source-backed conditions for flow before recommending immersion as the treatment.
evidence_required: A clear goal for the activity.
A rapid feedback loop for performance or drift.
Evidence that the challenge is high but not panic-inducing for the current skill level.
A reason sustained immersion is more important than fragmented multitasking.
good_output_shape: Names the goal, feedback loop, challenge level, and current skill before advising deep focus.
Explains why the task belongs in the flow channel instead of boredom, anxiety, or ordinary execution.
Protects uninterrupted concentration only after the flow conditions are visible.

treatment_requirements.description: Insert lightweight problem-fit checks so efficient immersion does not continue on the wrong problem or stale challenge.
evidence_required: The larger problem or purpose that the deep work is meant to serve.
The point at which the work should pause for big-picture review.
Evidence that the challenge still stretches ability instead of sliding into boredom or overwhelm.
good_output_shape: Includes a periodic check that the focused work still solves the ultimate problem.
Names the feedback or premise that should interrupt the session if the problem framing is wrong.
Adjusts the challenge upward, downward, or sideways when the skill-to-demand match changes.

good_output_shape: Names the feedback loop that should reinforce the desired behavior.
Avoids recommending goodwill, vague alignment, or motivational language as the main intervention.

case_evidence_needed: The task type: algorithmic, heuristic, creative, conceptual, or routine.
The current or proposed reward form, including if-then rewards, now-that rewards, autonomy, mastery, purpose, praise, feedback, or useful information.

Likely second-order effects such as gaming, avoidance, escalation, trust erosion, stifled inventiveness, or compliance without commitment.
do_not_use_when: The task type, desired behavior, reward mechanism, or measured target cannot be named.
The proposed intervention cannot observe or change the reward, measure, feedback loop, or engagement process.
The answer would use one generic incentive design without checking task fit, metric gaming, and second-order system effects.
use_when: A system, compensation plan, workflow, or governance process is being redesigned because current outputs are shaped by wrong local rewards.
The task may be algorithmic, heuristic, creative, or conceptual enough that reward type changes performance.
A metric, local incentive, or contingent reward could be gamed or create downstream avoidance, escalation, trust erosion, or loss of commitment.

diagnostic_questions: Is this task algorithmic enough for an if-then reward, or does it require flexible problem-solving, inventiveness, or creativity?
What will happen if the measured incentive becomes the target?

Does the incentive build genuine commitment, or only compliance with definitions, inducements, measures, and standards?
affordances.mechanism: When redesigning incentives, match the reward form to the task type, then test whether the measure becomes the target, creative work is crowded out, or local efficiency creates avoidance, escalation, or trust erosion.
misuse_guards: Do not use contingent if-then rewards for creative, right-brain, or heuristic tasks without checking for stifled inventiveness.
Do not treat local efficiency as system success until second-order avoidance, escalation, and trust erosion have been checked.
Do not rely solely on definitions, inducements, measures, and standards when the problem is commitment and accountability.

source_evidence.source_quote: **Best used when** redesigning a system, compensation plan, workflow, or governance process where the current outputs are being shaped by the wrong local rewards.
For sophisticated, conceptual, or **heuristic tasks** (typical of 21st-century work), traditional "carrot-and-stick" extrinsic motivators often fail or do harm.
Extrinsic **"if-then" rewards** (contingent on performance) are effective for **algorithmic tasks** that follow an existing formula to a logical conclusion.

for tasks demanding **flexible problem-solving, inventiveness, or creativity**, it is better to use **"now that" rewards**, which provide non-contingent praise, feedback, and useful information *after* the job is done.
**Danger when** the measured incentive becomes the target and people learn to game the metric while degrading the real objective.
**Danger when** local incentives look efficient in isolation but create second-order avoidance, escalation, or trust erosion elsewhere in the system.
Relying solely on definitions, inducements, measures, and standards reinforces a **bureaucratic mindset** and fails to build genuine commitment and accountability.

treatment_requirements.description: Classify the task before choosing the incentive form, because contingent rewards fit algorithmic work differently from creative or heuristic work.
evidence_required: Whether the task follows an existing formula or requires flexible problem-solving, inventiveness, creativity, or conceptual judgment.
The proposed or current reward structure, including whether it is contingent if-then reward or non-contingent now-that feedback.
Any intrinsic motivation levers such as autonomy, mastery, and purpose that are relevant to the task.
good_output_shape: Names the task type before recommending the incentive design.
Uses contingent if-then rewards only where the work is algorithmic enough to support them.
Uses autonomy, mastery, purpose, or now-that feedback when creative, heuristic, or conceptual work would be harmed by carrot-and-stick rewards.

treatment_requirements.description: Stress-test the proposed incentive for metric gaming, local optimization, second-order system effects, and compliance without genuine commitment.
evidence_required: The measured incentive and the real objective it might displace.

good_output_shape: States how the measure could become the target and degrade the real objective.
Traces downstream behavior beyond the first visible intervention.
Rejects incentive designs that create local efficiency at the cost of system-level trust, cooperation, creativity, or commitment.

absence_records.reason: The source does not support replacing information asymmetry with generic uncertainty or calibration. Its operational trigger is unequal relevant knowledge between parties, especially cases where one side controls information, proof, or observability.

source_evidence.source_quote: Information Asymmetry (IA) is fundamentally defined by the **unequal distribution of relevant knowledge** between two or more parties in a transaction, relationship, or decision.
Most useful when pricing, trust, or strategic choice hinges on hidden information, concealed incentives, or unshared operating realities.
Most useful when a vendor, outsourcer, or delegated operator supplies the core performance metrics and the plan is treating those numbers as if they were independent validation.

absence_records.reason: The source includes examples of exploiting asymmetry in private equity, persuasion, and FUD, but this reviewed pass does not have a policy-safe operational treatment standard for promoting those tactics. Keep exploitation material as review context rather than a runtime or Lane-4 behavior.

source_evidence.source_quote: Information Asymmetry is applied in the real world both as a strategic tool to exploit and as a vulnerability to mitigate through deliberate processes.
The PE investor’s worldview specifically assumes that **information asymmetries make it possible to capture opportunities others don’t see**.
This tactic exploits the asymmetry of knowledge regarding risk—telling the uninformed party (procurement) the potential areas of risk and consequences associated with prioritizing a low price.

case_evidence_needed: Which party is being evaluated and which metrics, references, pilots, dashboards, or scorecards that party controls.
The commitment decision that would change with disclosure, diligence, observability, signaling, or incentive redesign.
The hidden behaviors or payoff the other side could optimize for, such as margin, speed, commission extraction, risk transfer, discounting, exception handling, or overpromising.
do_not_use_when: Critical evidence is externally audited, directly observable in production, or contractually hard for the other side to manipulate.
The blocker is already known to be incentives, governance, or authority rather than missing party-specific information.
The team is asking for generic more information without naming a hidden variable that would change the decision.
use_when: Pricing, trust, or strategic choice hinges on hidden information, concealed incentives, or unshared operating realities between parties.
A vendor, outsourcer, agent, or delegated partner supplies the metrics, references, pilot outputs, or scorecards being treated as go/no-go proof.
Visible outcomes look strong, but the principal cannot observe the behaviors or tradeoffs that produced them.

diagnostic_questions: What specific metric, reference, or proof in this plan is controlled by the party trying to win the decision?
What demand signal exists that the partner has no incentive to influence -- and what would your scale decision look like if only that signal counted?
If the vendor or agent were optimizing for a different payoff than we assume, where would we see it first?
Which people know the most, which people bear the risk, and which people approve the decision?
affordances.mechanism: When the party being evaluated controls dashboards, references, pilots, or reported outcomes, treat the visible evidence as part of the asymmetry: name what the party can choose or game, identify the hidden payoff it may optimize, and require an independent signal or direct observability before commitment.
misuse_guards: Do not accept dashboards, references, or pilot outputs as independent proof when the party being evaluated chose what to reveal, how to measure it, and under which conditions.
Do not ask for generic more information without naming the missing fact that would change price, trust, sequencing, or go/no-go choice.
Do not reach for information asymmetry when the critical evidence is externally audited, directly observable in production, or contractually hard to manipulate.
Do not collapse the model into generic uncertainty or calibration; preserve who controls which evidence and who bears the decision risk.
affordances.name: Party-controlled evidence requires observability redesign

dropped_material: Did not split vendor dashboards, partner bookings, references, pilots, and hidden payoff into separate affordances because they share one evidence-control mechanism.
Did not turn principal-agent language into a separate model relation; the source supports it here only as the party-level shape of the information asymmetry.
review_notes.normalization_note: This affordance preserves the Lane-4-frequent party-level pattern: who controls the evidence, who benefits from that control, and what independent observability would change the decision.
open_questions: A future relation review may decide whether some hidden-agent material should be delegated to principal-agent-problem rather than remaining inside this affordance.

source_evidence.source_quote: Information Asymmetry (IA) is fundamentally defined by the **unequal distribution of relevant knowledge** between two or more parties in a transaction, relationship, or decision.
Most useful when pricing, trust, or strategic choice hinges on hidden information, concealed incentives, or unshared operating realities.
Works when the practical decision is whether to add diligence, require disclosure, redesign incentives, use signaling, or map the real decision-makers before committing.
Best when the people who know the most, the people who bear the risk, and the people who approve the decision are not the same actors.
Most useful when a vendor, outsourcer, or delegated operator supplies the core performance metrics and the plan is treating those numbers as if they were independent validation.
Works when the evidence base comes from hand-picked references, staged proofs of concept, or sandbox conditions controlled by the party trying to win the deal.
Best when a partner, agent, or service provider can privately optimize for speed, margin, commission extraction, or risk transfer in ways the principal cannot directly see.
Works when booked revenue, turnaround time, or quality dashboards look good but the plan has not tested whether the measurement system itself can be gamed.

Most useful when supplier scorecards, implementation dashboards, customer references, or pilot benchmarks are being used as go/no-go proof even though the vendor chose the measures and the conditions.
Works when revenue, turnaround, or SLA numbers look strong but the principal cannot observe discounting, exception handling, overpromising, claim denials, or other behaviors generating those results.
A major anti-pattern is asking for “more information” without naming what missing fact would actually change price, trust, sequencing, or go/no-go choice.
A common mistake is accepting dashboards, references, or PoC outputs as independent proof even though the party being evaluated chose what to reveal, how to measure it, and under which conditions.
Avoid reaching for information asymmetry when the critical evidence is externally audited, directly observable in production, or contractually hard to manipulate.
The principal then scales the channel on signals the partner controls instead of signals the market independently confirms.
What demand signal exists that the partner has no incentive to influence -- and what would your scale decision look like if only that signal counted?
Require direct observability on discounting, customer quality, and downstream performance before expanding.

treatment_requirements.description: Before accepting visible performance, identify every dashboard, metric, reference, pilot, benchmark, or scorecard controlled by the party trying to win the decision and mark it as self-selected until independently observed.
evidence_required: The party being judged and the proof that party supplies.
Which measures, conditions, references, or reporting channels that party chose or can shape.
The current decision being made from those visible signals.
good_output_shape: Separates independent evidence from party-controlled evidence.
Names the specific proof that could be selected, staged, gamed, or reported under favorable conditions.
Refuses to treat supplier-designed proof as neutral go/no-go evidence.

treatment_requirements.description: Require at least one demand, quality, risk, or performance signal that the evaluated party has little incentive or ability to influence, then state how the scale, trust, pricing, or oversight decision changes if only that signal counts.
evidence_required: The independent signal that would validate demand, quality, risk, or performance.
How the evaluated party could influence the current visible signal.
The decision threshold or commitment change if only the independent signal counted.
good_output_shape: Identifies a signal outside the other party's control.
States the decision that would follow from the independent signal alone.
Delays scaling or commitment until direct observability exists on the behaviors producing the visible outcome.

treatment_requirements.description: Ask what payoff the vendor, agent, partner, or service provider could privately optimize for, where that behavior would show up first, and what observability or oversight would catch it before commitment.
evidence_required: The role of the other party: vendor, agent, partner, service provider, outsourcer, or delegated operator.
The private payoff that could differ from the principal's assumed payoff.
The first observable traces of that hidden behavior.
good_output_shape: Names the hidden optimization that would change trust, pricing, or oversight.
Connects the hidden optimization to observable behaviors such as discounting, exception handling, overpromising, claim denials, commission extraction, or risk transfer.
Proposes observability or oversight changes before relying on the reported result.

case_evidence_needed: The informed party and the recipient or novice whose understanding is incomplete.
Where the current communication is too abstract, too detailed, or not actionable for the decision recipient.
Stories, timelines, decisions, precursors, actions, results, interpretations, and the simple core message that would let the recipient act.
do_not_use_when: The evidence problem is supplier-controlled proof or hidden optimization; use the party-controlled evidence affordance instead of treating it as communication.
The recipient already has the concrete steps, timeline, and decision path, and the blocker is authority or incentives to act on known facts.
A full accurate explanation would overwhelm the recipient; keep the output centered on the actionable core.
use_when: An expert is communicating in abstract, high-level terms while the audience needs concrete details or an actionable core.
The task depends on tacit knowledge that the expert has difficulty articulating because they rely on recognition and intuition.
A recipient needs to understand the model, decision path, or message well enough to act, not merely receive a conclusion.

diagnostic_questions: How does the expert think about the problem?
What do you think the other side doesn’t 'get' about your perspective?
How will the audience absorb the content?
Which story, timeline, precursor, action, result, or interpretation would reveal the decision process?
affordances.mechanism: When an informed party's expertise leaves a novice, frontline worker, or decision recipient with abstractions instead of usable understanding, convert the asymmetry into inspectable process: ask how the expert thinks, collect stories, timelines, and step-by-step choices, then compress the result to the actionable core the recipient can use.
misuse_guards: Do not accept expert abstraction when the audience needs concrete details.
Do not ask only what to do when that hides the underlying model the recipient needs.
Do not let full accuracy become useless by making the communication too complex for decision or action.
Do not confuse exhaustive fact gathering with the raw material needed to expose the expert's decision process.
affordances.name: Expert-recipient gaps require tacit process extraction

dropped_material: Did not turn all presentation and teaching examples into separate affordances because the source's treatment requirement is the same: make the hidden expert model concrete and accessible.
Did not promote relation-only Feynman Technique or cognitive-load material here; those are already represented in relation curation.
review_notes.normalization_note: This keeps the expert-recipient side of the source as an operational extraction problem: reveal the informed party's process and translate the core for the recipient.
open_questions: Review may later decide that expert-recipient communication belongs in a narrower communication model rather than in information-asymmetry affordances.

source_evidence.source_quote: **To diagnose communication failures** that arise when experts fail to account for the novice's incomplete understanding (the "Curse of Knowledge").
This curse leads experts to communicate in abstract, high-level terms when the audience needs concrete details.
An accurate but useless idea is still useless.
The IA exists because the expert has all the precise, useless information, while the frontline worker needs the simple, actionable core.
When seeking advice, rather than asking *what* to do (which yields an abstraction), ask *how* the expert thinks about the problem.
This "deepens your understanding" by revealing the underlying model.
Experts often possess **tacit knowledge**—knowledge that is difficult to articulate because they rely on recognition and intuition.
Focusing on facts, timelines, and walking through decisions step-by-step, like a journalist, provides raw material for investigation.

Having experts generate typical problems, and then having them think aloud while solving peer problems (Precursor, Action, Result, Interpretation) helps illuminate the basic flow of the problem-solving process.

An expert’s instinct is to communicate the full, highly detailed, and accurate answer, but if this information is too complex, it fails to help the recipient make decisions or take action, making the idea useless.
Experts unintentionally communicate abstractly and opaquely because they cannot recall what it is like to lack their specialized knowledge.
Focus on the **core message** and avoid exhaustive, distracting facts.
The receiving party (or novice) is overwhelmed by excessive, intricate data presented by the informed party and loses focus on the actionable core.

treatment_requirements.description: When advice arrives as abstraction, ask how the expert thinks about the problem so the underlying model becomes visible instead of taking only the recommendation.
evidence_required: The recommendation or conclusion the expert has offered.

good_output_shape: Elicits the expert's thinking process rather than only the answer.
Names the underlying model or decision path behind the advice.
Converts the expert's abstraction into concrete information the recipient can use.

treatment_requirements.description: Use cognitive task analysis by collecting stories, timelines, and step-by-step decisions, then ask for precursors, actions, results, and interpretations so tacit knowledge becomes inspectable.
evidence_required: A real story, incident, or typical problem from the expert.
The timeline and step-by-step decisions taken.
The precursor, action, result, and interpretation that shaped the expert's judgment.
good_output_shape: Grounds the exchange in stories and facts rather than abstract advice.
Shows the flow of the expert's problem-solving process.
Makes recognition-based tacit judgment explicit enough for review or transfer.

treatment_requirements.description: Translate the expert's full accurate knowledge into a simple actionable core, using concrete comparisons or analogies where they help the recipient make a decision.
evidence_required: The accurate but too-complex explanation currently being offered.
The recipient's context, capacity, and decision need.
The core message or comparison that would make action possible.
good_output_shape: Keeps the useful core while dropping exhaustive distracting facts.
Uses concrete language or analogy to create a usable mental bucket for the recipient.
Separates detailed backup material from the simple message needed for action.

dropped_material: Did not create separate affordances for PE investing, pitching, procurement FUD, WYSIATI, Feynman Technique, cognitive load theory, or Systems Thinking because those either need policy review, belong to relation curation, or duplicate another model's mechanism.

Did not create a generic unknown-unknowns or calibration affordance; the source's usable IA shape is one party possessing, withholding, selecting, or failing to articulate information another party needs.

case_evidence_needed: The desired outcome, plan, or commitment being considered.

diagnostic_questions: What don't I want to achieve? What causes the non-goal? How can I avoid that?

source_evidence.source_quote: Instead of asking how to achieve a desired outcome, inversion demands that you ask what would guarantee failure, and then systematically avoid those things.
the cost of a mistake is catastrophic, making the avoidance of stupidity a far more profitable strategy than the pursuit of brilliance.
the path to success is unclear, but the paths to failure are identifiable.
Rather than setting direct goals, establish "anti-goals." Ask yourself: "What don't I want to achieve? What causes the non-goal? How can I avoid that?"
Ask the team: "What actions could our company take to destroy as much value as possible in as short a time as possible?" Then ensure those behaviors are eradicated.
identifying what to avoid without subsequently taking positive action leads to paralysis, not progress.
"straw man" negative scenarios are generated that are easily dismissed, falsely convincing the team they have applied rigorous backward thinking when they have merely performed a ritual.
A team might identify the five ways a project could fail, implement safeguards for those five things, and falsely believe the system is now foolproof

source_evidence.source_quote: When forming an initial assumption or hypothesis, rather than looking for evidence to confirm it, use inversion to actively seek ways to disprove your own theory.
Charles Darwin famously utilized this extreme anti-confirmation-bias method, training himself to intensively consider any evidence that tended to disconfirm his hypotheses.
By taking your initial assumption and asking "Let's try and disprove it," inversion forces a level of objectivity that forward-thinking rarely achieves.
Where can I find the evidence that suggests I am wrong?
Require at least one inverse scenario that directly threatens the team's preferred plan, not just peripheral risks.
inversion conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when backward analysis is subconsciously constrained to scenarios that still validate the preferred forward plan.
the emotional investment in being right overrides the intellectual discipline of checking for wrong.

source_evidence.source_quote: seeking and integrating external perspectives helps the individual or team **calibrate** their judgment to better reflect reality.

**Most useful when blind spots are materially affecting coordination or trust:** The model is strongest when better outcomes depend on surfacing what others can see that the individual or team cannot.

**Works when feedback and disclosure can change execution quality, not just feelings:** It is a high-leverage fit in coaching, management, and collaboration settings where hidden misunderstandings create recurring friction.
**Best when the group needs structured self-awareness rather than vague openness:** The model adds value when teams can turn reflection into specific behavior change.

The Johari Window is a descriptive psychological model. The sources caution that it is **"not meant to be a standalone guide"** for inner world understanding and should be used vis-à-vis other personality models.

The tool requires specific, honest input. If used with generic or "socially acceptable" feedback, it risks becoming an exercise in **"illusion of knowing"**—mistaking fluency with the process for actual mastery or insight.
The model fails when people share curated, socially safe observations instead of the specific feedback or self-revelation needed to reduce the real blind spot.
johari-window conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when feedback intake is filtered to protect existing self-image.

Use language that is **"Descriptive, Focused, Specific, Brief, and Simple"** when providing feedback to avoid triggering a defensive response.

case_evidence_needed: The defined problem and a logical disaggregation of the relevant system pieces.

do_not_use_when: A preferred initiative is being called a leverage point before its feedback loop, bottleneck, or governing rule has been proved.
The team is jumping into data or complex analysis before defining the underlying problem structure.
The candidate issue may be high impact, but the actor cannot actually influence it.
use_when: A system has many moving parts but only a few intervention points are likely to change behavior materially.
Resources are limited and one action must alter incentives, flow, feedback, or constraints disproportionately.

diagnostic_questions: Which few intervention points are likely to change behavior materially?

affordances.mechanism: Good use isolates the few influenceable system variables that can change behavior materially, then requires proof that the proposed action sits near an actual feedback loop, bottleneck, governing rule, incentive, flow, or constraint before it earns priority.

affordances.name: Prove the structural leverage point before action

source_evidence.source_quote: The essence of the model is that not all interventions are created equal; some points within a system offer disproportionately high impact for the effort applied.

Effective use requires breaking the problem apart into logical pieces and structuring the components, often using a **logic tree**. Problem solvers should focus on issues that have the biggest impact and that they can actually **influence**.
The seven-step problem-solving process begins with precisely defining the problem, disaggregating issues, and **prioritizing what to do and what not to do**.
• **Most useful when** the system has many moving parts but only a few intervention points are likely to change behavior materially.
• **Works best when** resources are limited and the team must decide where one action can alter incentives, flow, feedback, or constraints disproportionately.
• **Best used when** repeated effort at the surface has failed and the real opportunity lies deeper in the system structure.
• **Danger when** leaders label every preferred initiative a leverage point without proving that it sits near a real feedback loop, bottleneck, or governing rule.
• **Jumping to Analysis:** Wading prematurely into data and complex analysis (even with AI techniques) without first clearly defining the structure of the underlying problem is a common error.

treatment_requirements.description: Define the problem, disaggregate the system, and identify the few high-impact influenceable points before recommending an intervention.

Separates high-impact influenceable issues from attractive but low-leverage work.

misuse_guards: Do not boil the ocean by gathering facts that cannot support or refute the central analysis.
Do not keep producing charts in pursuit of tiny marginal gains when the effort is disproportionate to the gain.

This ensures data collection is highly focused and efficient.
The process of identifying and acting on leverage points must be constantly monitored to ensure focus remains on high-impact areas.
◦ "How does what you’re doing solve the problem?".
◦ "How does it advance your thinking?".
◦ "Is it the most important thing you could be doing right now?".

• **Boiling the Ocean:** This anti-pattern involves gathering a mountain of data or continually producing more charts in pursuit of tiny, marginal gains, ignoring the fact that the audience cannot absorb more and that the effort is disproportionate to the gain.

case_evidence_needed: The assumptions and underlying logic implicit in the proposed leverage point.
Disconfirming evidence, an antithesis, outside-view evidence, or an opposing perspective that could challenge the proposal.
The expected resistance, worst-case scenario, and execution path that converts the chosen leverage point from what to how.
do_not_use_when: There is no candidate leverage point or system intervention to harden yet.

The action is a simple low-resistance task where system pushback and leverage-point capture are not material.
use_when: A proposed intervention targets deep structure, governing paradigms, or another high-resistance part of the system.

misuse_guards: Do not let a familiar framework turn a novel context into an assumed X problem.

affordances.name: Harden leverage choices against resistance and bias

source_evidence.source_quote: A key principle associated with Leverage Points is that **the higher the leverage point, the more the system will resist changing it**.

It is often straightforward to identify the necessary strategy (the "what," or the leverage point), but extremely difficult to figure out the **execution** (the "how"). A great plan that cannot be implemented is useless.

While simplification is necessary to manage complexity, there is a risk that using pre-existing theoretical frameworks leads to the assumption, "I have seen this one before, it is an X problem," which may be misleading in a novel context.

• **Maslow's Hammer (Law of the Instrument):** This cognitive bias is a direct antagonist. It occurs when an individual, possessing expertise in one area, attempts to treat every problem as if it were solvable only with their single available tool. This prevents the user from recognizing that the highest-leverage intervention might exist outside their specialized domain.
| **Confirmation Bias** | Focusing analysis only on data that supports the initial assumed leverage point, rather than seeking **disconfirming evidence** or flaws in the underlying logic. |  |

| **Motivated Reasoning** | Allowing personal beliefs or emotional investments (e.g., career implications of disproving a boss's vision) to steer the diagnosis away from uncovering the true, uncomfortable problem. |  |

| **Dialectic Standard & Constructive Confrontation** | Establish a norm where every hypothesis or idea is met with its **antithesis** before synthesis. Ask **“What would you have to believe?”** to explicitly spell out and stress-test all assumptions implicit in the proposed leverage point. |  |

| **Anchoring to the Outside View** | Counter the inside view (your own perspective and biases) by anchoring decision-making to the **outside view**—what is true of the world in general or how others would see the situation. |  |

| **Pre-mortem and Risk Quantification** | Use pre-mortems (or game theory thinking) to model competitive scenarios, assess likely payoffs, and simulate outcomes. Explicitly ask: **"Is the worst case bad enough?"** to ensure sufficient weight is given to outcomes far from the expected mean. |  |

treatment_requirements.description: Expose the assumptions carrying the proposed leverage point and test them against antithesis, disconfirming evidence, or an outside view.

good_output_shape: Separates the strategic what from the execution how.

Includes a worst-case check before aggressive leverage is pursued.

The model provides a structured way to think about how different elements within a complex system can be modified to produce desired outcomes, aiming for **system change**.

source_evidence.source_quote: If two theories make the same predictions, OR dictates preferring the one with the least number of unproven assumptions until further evidence is available.
Occam's Razor is strongest when several stories could explain the situation and the immediate job is to prune gratuitous assumptions before committing to a more elaborate theory.
OR is particularly effective when a lean explanation improves diagnosis, communication, or prioritization while still preserving the essential causal structure.
The razor is misused when people prefer the cleanest story mainly because it feels easier, calmer, or more elegant rather than because it actually fits the strongest facts.

case_evidence_needed: Evidence that the current frame is binary or prematurely narrowed.
A list of at least three viable options before evaluation begins.
A comparison basis for benefits, costs, payoffs, or probabilities across options.
do_not_use_when: The option set is already large enough that further expansion is causing paralysis.
The decision is routine or inconsequential enough that option analysis would waste attention.
The team is seeking a perfect option instead of making a needed decision.
use_when: An important decision is framed as only do or do not.
The problem is complex enough that premature selection may hide better alternatives.
The current recommendation lacks a visible array of viable paths.

diagnostic_questions: What viable third option breaks the do-or-do-not frame?
Have we generated at least three options before selecting among them?
Which benefits, costs, payoffs, or probabilities differ across the options?
affordances.mechanism: When an important decision has collapsed into a do-or-do-not frame, separate invention from evaluation: generate at least three viable options, then compare benefits, costs, payoffs, and probabilities before choosing.
misuse_guards: Stop expanding the set when too many attractive choices are preventing any decision.
Do not spend meaningful analysis on routine, inconsequential choices.
Do not keep analyzing in pursuit of a perfect option when action is needed.
affordances.name: Create a non-binary option set before selection

source_evidence.source_quote: the tendency to simplify a complex issue down to just two options (e.g., "do" or "do not").
requiring the decision-maker to generate at least three options (3+) for every important decision.
generate many options before selecting among them
requires quantifying benefits and costs across an array of options.
decision trees can be used, particularly if some choices are probabilistic in nature.

The goal is not to analyze everything to achieve perfection

treatment_requirements.description: Delay selection long enough to invent multiple viable paths, with at least three options visible before the recommendation narrows.
evidence_required: Current binary framing or prematurely narrowed alternatives.
A generated option set with at least three viable paths.
Criteria that explain why any option is retained or eliminated.
good_output_shape: Names the expanded option set before recommending a path.
Explains which options remain viable and which are dominated.
Shows that selection happened after invention, not before it.

treatment_requirements.description: Evaluate the option array by comparing benefits, costs, payoffs, and probabilistic branches rather than using a simple pro-con list.
evidence_required: Benefits and costs for each serious option.
Relevant payoffs, probabilities, or branches where uncertainty matters.
Explicit trade-offs that distinguish the options from each other.
good_output_shape: Presents a compact comparison across the option array.
Identifies the option with the best supported upside-to-downside profile.
Names uncertainty that should change the final selection.

case_evidence_needed: A clear statement of what makes the choice reversible or irreversible.
A cheap experiment or staged action that buys information before final commitment.
A downside cap and a decision boundary for when enough information exists to commit.
do_not_use_when: Preserving options delays the commitment needed to capture time-sensitive value.
Maintaining optionality consumes cash, attention, or simplicity better spent on the chosen path.
The worst case is too severe to tolerate even as a staged experiment.
use_when: Uncertainty is high, early commitment is expensive, and cheap experiments can buy learning.
The decision is reversible enough that moving now creates information value.
Downside can be capped while upside remains open across plausible futures.

diagnostic_questions: What cheap reversible move buys information before the irreversible call?
Is the worst case bad enough?
Would we regret making or not making this decision later?
Do we have between 40% and 70% of the information needed to decide?
affordances.mechanism: When uncertainty is high and commitment is expensive, keep several reversible paths open long enough to run cheap learning moves, cap downside, and commit once evidence makes delay more costly than choice.
misuse_guards: Do not let optionality become a permanent excuse not to commit after evidence clarifies best fit.
Do not preserve paths whose carrying cost is consuming cash, attention, or simplicity.
Do not treat every decision as irreversible; that assumption can create indecision.
affordances.name: Preserve reversible paths to buy learning
